issue_title: remove getter functions
issue_body:
(Copied from [[https://sourceforge.net/p/python-irclib/feature-requests/4/|SourceForge]])

They are horribly ugly and unpythonic.
Instead of e.g. whatever.source().xyz it should be whatever.source.xyz.
The easiest way to change this while keeping the read-only style of those properties is simply decorating all those getters with @property and then using them like normal variables.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/irc/issue/2
- Originally reported by: Jason R. Coombs
- Originally created at: 2012-09-29T12:13:39.111

issue_title: Wrong hiding when using multiple message types and labels
issue_body:
## Expected Behavior  
I have two message types, one label in the first, and two labels in the second.  
Hiding the label in the first message type should hide only the labelled bits, and only of that message type.  

## Actual Behavior  
If a label say "bit 1-31" bits 1-31 are hidden regardless of the message type

## Steps to Reproduce the Problem

 1. Add two message types and one label per message type  
 2. set two rows as two different message types  
 3. hide one label  
 4. (part of) the other is hidden too  

Check the photo:  
![bug](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20688006/40673754-fd8a748e-6372-11e8-82bb-16e997608038.png)


## Platform Specifications

  - Python Version: installed 3.6 but it uses 3.5 included with the msi
  - Operating System: win8.1 x64
  - Version of URH: 2.0.4 MSI installer x64
  - URH was installed: via msi

issue_title: TornadoSession.validate enters endless loop upon failure
issue_body:
Not completely sure what is going on here, but when `TornadoSession.validate` is called with a bad password it fails with an exception which is fine except that something is left on the ioloop that eats the CPU alive.  The following script will show the problem when you run it with a DSN that is valid except for the password:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
#
import logging
import signal
import sys

import queries
from tornado import gen, ioloop


@gen.coroutine
def main():
    logger = logging.getLogger('main')
    for dsn in sys.argv[1:]:
        logger.info('starting session with dsn=%s', dsn)
        try:
            session = queries.TornadoSession(dsn)
            yield session.validate()
            logger.info('validated')
        except:
            logger.exception('FAILURE FOR %s', dsn)

    while True:
        yield gen.sleep(10)


def sig_handler(*args):
    logging.info('signal handler invoked with %r', args)
    iol = ioloop.IOLoop.instance()
    iol.add_callback_from_signal(iol.stop)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    if len(sys.argv) == 1:
        print('Usage:', sys.argv[0], 'DSN [DSN...]')
        sys.exit(1)

    logging.basicConfig(
        level=logging.DEBUG,
        format='[%(process)d] %(levelname)1.1s - %(name)s: %(message)s')
    signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, sig_handler)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, sig_handler)
    iol = ioloop.IOLoop.instance()
    iol.add_future(main(), lambda _: None)
    iol.start()
```

When given a valid DSN it will validate the session and sleep until you kill with Ctrl+C.  Give it an invalid password and it will report and error and go into the same sleep cycle with something like the following log:
```
(env) Daves-MBP/queries-play% ./validate.py 'postgresql://postgres:secret2@127.0.0.1:32770/postgres'
[20562] I - main: starting session with dsn=postgresql://postgres:secret2@127.0.0.1:32770/postgres
[20562] D - queries.pool: Creating Pool: ea2750c53184e9ddec80659446cbe6fa (60/25)
[20562] D - queries.tornado_session: Creating a new connection for ea2750c53184e9ddec80659446cbe6fa
[20562] E - main: FAILURE FOR postgresql://postgres:secret2@127.0.0.1:32770/postgres
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./validate.py", line 18, in main
    yield session.validate()
  File "/Users/daveshawley/Source/python/queries-play/env/lib/python3.4/site-packages/tornado/gen.py", line 1015, in run
    value = future.result()
  File "/Users/daveshawley/Source/python/queries-play/env/lib/python3.4/site-packages/tornado/concurrent.py", line 237, in result
    raise_exc_info(self._exc_info)
  File "<string>", line 3, in raise_exc_info
  File "/Users/daveshawley/Source/python/queries-play/env/lib/python3.4/site-packages/queries/tornado_session.py", line 460, in _poll_connection
    state = self._connections[fd].poll()
psycopg2.OperationalError: FATAL:  password authentication failed for user "postgres"

```

Now run top or similar process monitor and you'll notice that the process is consuming an entire core.  It is not chewing through memory or stranding sockets (verified by `lsof`).  It is simply looping very hard on something.  I ran `sudo dtrace -p 20562 -n 'syscall:::entry'` to see what was going on and it looks like it might be failing to handle a socket error and retrying the connection sequence repeatedly.  The dtrace output settles to:
```
  4    883           workq_kernreturn:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  6    873                     kevent:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  0    333                     select:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  6    873                     kevent:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
  2    941             write_nocancel:entry
```
repeatedly.  There are no calls to IO traps so I don't think that it is trying a new connection.  It looks like it is simply looping repeatedly.

issue_title: Add --cache-only flag
issue_body: Instead of having CI run the full mutation stage we could check in the entire mutmut cache dir and in CI run with a a --cache-only flag where it fails if the cache is out of date. This would make it nice to have a mutation testing badge on your project on github, but without the hassle of storing and loading the mutmut cache database or the cost of running the full mutation testing on travis on each commit.

issue_title: zh_CN (and possible other providers) return invalid results for domain_name
issue_body:
Brief summary of the issue goes here.

### Steps to reproduce

f = Faker('zh_CN')
f.domain_name()
>he.cn

### Expected behavior

it should return valid domain names

### Actual behavior

he.cn is not a valid domain. he is in the list of second-level domains (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/.cn#Second-level_domains_of_provinces) and will cause failures in places where domains are being validated.

it's unclear where this change should/could be made after briefly reviewing the source code.

issue_title: Use threading, queue with ldap3 to query Active Directory
issue_body:
Hello, I have been attempting to get a program working that can query Active Directory as quickly as possible. I believe I have understood the general idea of how to use ldap3 to query the AD as when I do it in a single thread it works fine. Now I would like to speed up the process using threading and queues but have run into issues using the REUSABLE connection. Below you'll find the code I am trying to get working.

```
import ldap3 as ldap
from queue import Queue
import threading

def call(q, c):
    while True:
        dns_name = q.get()
        if dns_name is None:
            break

        if c is None:
            print('Connection Failed')
            break

        search_base = 'company.domain.string.local'
    
        search_base_split = ','.join(['DC=' + x for x in search_base.split('.')])

            params = {
                'search_base': search_base_split,
                'search_filter': '(' + 'CN=' + dns_name + ' )',
                'search_scope': ldap.SUBTREE,
                # 'time_limit': '60'
            }

            try:
                c.bind()
                search_id = c.search(**params)
                if search_id:
                    response = c.get_response(search_id, timeout=60)
                    if len(response[0]) > 0:
                        if len(response[0]) > 1:
                            print(response[0][0]['dn'])
                c.unbind()
            except Exception as e:
                print(e)

        q.task_done()

def ldap_practice_main():
    with open('resources/dns_names.txt', 'r') as f:
        dns_names = f.readlines()

    q = Queue(maxsize=0)

    s = ldap.Server(
        'company.local',
        use_ssl=True,
        # get_info=ldap.ALL
    )

    num_threads = 10

    user = my_username
    password = my_password

    with ldap.Connection(
        s,
        user=user,
        password=password,
        # auto_bind=ldap.AUTO_BIND_NONE,
        authentication=ldap.SIMPLE,
        client_strategy=ldap.REUSABLE,
        collect_usage=True,
        read_only=True,
        lazy=False,
        raise_exceptions=True,
        pool_size=num_threads,
        pool_lifetime=120,
        pool_name='test_pool'
        # pool_keepalive=60,
        # receive_timeout=True,
    ) as c:

        for i in range(num_threads):
            thread = threading.Thread(target=call, args=[q, c])
            thread.setDaemon(True)
            thread.start()

    for dns_name in dns_names:
        q.put(dns_name.strip())

    q.join()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    try:
        ldap_practice_main()
    except Exception as e:
        print(str(e))
```

This is a somewhat simplified version of the code I am using but the idea is the same. I run into various errors when I run it. Some threads work fine and return the information I expect, and then others randomly throw a bunch of different errors, such as LDAPMaximumRetriesError, LDAPSocketReceiveError, LDAPOperationsErrorResult, LDAPSocketOpenError.

I'm not sure if I've understood correctly how to use a REUSABLE connection pool and open multiple connections simultaneously on the same server to search for different dns names. Any guidance you could provide would be very helpful. I can also supply the longer error messages I get.

issue_title: Different between Facts and Events
issue_body:
First of all, I would like to thank you for your great effort on developing this amazing framework. I am planning to use this in my own project.

I have tried the fraud detection example as below
```python
from durable.lang import *
with ruleset('fraud_detection'):

    @when_all(c.first << m.amount > 100,
              c.second << m.amount > c.first.amount * 2,
              c.third << m.amount > (c.first.amount + c.second.amount) / 2)
    def detected(c): 
        print('fraud detected -> {0} {1}'.format(c.first.amount, c.first.id))
        print('               -> {0} {1}'.format(c.second.amount, c.second.id))
        print('               -> {0} {1}'.format(c.third.amount, c.third.id))

    @when_start
    def start(host):
        sid = 18
        host.post('fraud_detection', {'id': 30, 'sid': sid, 'amount': 120})
        host.post('fraud_detection', {'id': 50, 'sid': sid, 'amount': 1000})

run_all()
```
It works as I expected with the following output
```
fraud detected -> 120 30
               -> 1000 50
               -> 1000 50
```
As far as I know, the difference between facts and events is the life cycle. Each event will only trigger one single action and be retracted automatically after action dispatched, while facts will stay in the same inference set until someone manually retract them. Therefore, I am expecting to see the same result as above even if I assert facts instead of posting events. 

I change the source code to this
```python
from durable.lang import *
with ruleset('fraud_detection'):

    @when_all(c.first << m.amount > 100,
              c.second << m.amount > c.first.amount * 2,
              c.third << m.amount > (c.first.amount + c.second.amount) / 2)
    def detected(c): 
        print('fraud detected -> {0} {1}'.format(c.first.amount, c.first.id))
        print('               -> {0} {1}'.format(c.second.amount, c.second.id))
        print('               -> {0} {1}'.format(c.third.amount, c.third.id))

    @when_start
    def start(host):
        sid = 18
        host.assert_fact('fraud_detection', {'id': 30, 'sid': sid, 'amount': 120})
        host.assert_fact('fraud_detection', {'id': 50, 'sid': sid, 'amount': 1000})

run_all()
```
In this case, there is no output at all. 
Could you please share some light on this case if I misunderstand anything.

issue_title: Evaluate rules against object (Ruby)
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm working with custom objects in Ruby and I need to evaluate against some of the object properties in order to fire the rules, but the rules don't fire.

Let's say we have this Ruby class:

```
class TestClass
  def initialize(number_value = 0, boolean_value = false, string_value = '', array_value = [])
    @number_value = number_value
    @boolean_value = boolean_value
    @string_value = string_value
    @array_value = array_value
  end
  attr_accessor :number_value, :boolean_value, :string_value, :array_value
end
```

And we want to execute this flow:

```
Durable.flowchart :flow do
  stage :input
  to :init, when_all(m.subject.matches('.*'))

  stage :init do
    puts 'init'
    obj = Oj.load(m.subject.delete('\\'))
    obj.boolean_value = true
    s.subject = obj
  end
  to :second_step, when_all(s.subject.boolean_value == true)

  stage :second_step do
    puts 'second step'
  end

  when_start do
    post :flow, {:id => 1, :sid => 1, :subject => create_objects}
  end

end

def create_objects
  obj = TestClass.new(1,false,'object 1', ['array1','array2'])
  parsed_json = Oj.dump(obj)
  puts parsed_json
  parsed_json
end

Durable.run_all
```

If I debug this execution, the value of s.subject at the end of  **:init** is in fact the object with boolean_value = true, but **:second_step** is never executed. 

Is there a way to execute this flow or the value to be evaluated should be in the **s** variable like **s.boolean_value = true**?

Thanks.

issue_title: Python 3 - Allow ScryptFile mode 'r' as long as underlying file is 'rb'.
issue_body:
Just an idea for a future enhancement.

Basically on Python 3, the binary file must be opened with the 'rb' mode and I check that the ScryptFile mode matches the file pointer mode.

Since Python 3 makes a distinction between what things like `read()` return, (bytes for 'rb', strings for 'r') it would be convenient to have the underlying file object opened as 'rb' (which it must be, since the encrypted file is binary) but specify the ScryptFile mode as 'r'. Then read could perform whatever text conversion the built-in file object performs in the case of 'r' vs 'rb'.

```py
queue = Queue.PriorityQueue()
def worker(f):

  for pkt in data:
    queue.put((pkt.sniff_time, pkt))
```

`for pkt in data` occasionally (not every time) throws an `AttributeError` with the message `NoneType object has no attribute get_debug`

I'm used to working with AutoHotkey keyboard shorcuts, I want to reproduce it with your library. However I'm facing some problems and for example the following script does not work as expected.

```
import keyboard
keyboard.add_hotkey('alt gr+a', lambda : keyboard.write('@'), suppress=True)
keyboard.add_hotkey('alt+j', lambda : keyboard.send('left'), suppress=True)
keyboard.wait()
```

I run it using pythonw. In this case :
-'alt gr+a' does not write anything.
-'alt+j' has the same effect as 'alt+j+left', as if "suppress=True" was not doing his job.

issue_title: modify_mass module error
issue_body:
Hi all, 

I'm interested to use the Reboundx module "modify_mass". 
Just to check if works in my computer I copied the ipython example and run: 

```
import rebound
import reboundx
import numpy as np
sim = rebound.Simulation()
sim.G = 4*np.pi**2 # use units of AU, yrs and solar masses

M0 = 1. # initial mass of star
sim.add(m=M0)
sim.add(a=1.)
sim.add(a=2.)
sim.add(a=3.)
sim.move_to_com()
ps = sim.particles

rebx = reboundx.Extras(sim)
mass_effect = rebx.add("modify_mass")

ps[0].params["tau_mass"] = -1.e4

Nout = 1000
mass = np.zeros(Nout)
times = np.linspace(0., 1.e4, Nout)

for i, time in enumerate(times):
    sim.integrate(time)
    mass[i] = sim.particles[0].m
```

issue_title: freezegun crashes with cryptography 1.2 because of integer dict keys
issue_body:
Hello,

I originally filed this on cryptography, but they said it was probably more appropriate for freezegun or cffi.  Please let me know if you think it's more appropriate for cffi.  Thank you!

Cheers,
Fitz

Original bug report:
----

With the release of cryptography-1.2.0, my freezegun-using tests have started crashing.  The traceback looks like:

````
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "breaker2.py", line 10, in <module>
    with freezegun.freeze_time('2015-10-31 12:00:01'):
  File "/Users/fitz/.virtualenvs/cgdb/lib/python3.4/site-packages/freezegun/api.py", line 321, in __enter__
    self.start()
  File "/Users/fitz/.virtualenvs/cgdb/lib/python3.4/site-packages/freezegun/api.py", line 374, in start
    for module_attribute in dir(module):
TypeError: unorderable types: str() < int()
````

The crash occurs on [this line](https://github.com/spulec/freezegun/blob/master/freezegun/api.py#L375) when freezegun attempts to `dir()` on `_cffi_backend._extern_py`.  That module has integer keys in its `__dict__` method, causing the above error.  The dump of the `__dict__` looks like this:

````
{4441887152: (<ctype 'void(*)(int, int, char *, int)'>,
              <bound method type._lock_cb of <class 'cryptography.hazmat.bindings.openssl.binding.Binding'>>,
              b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00',
              None),
 4441887184: (<ctype 'int(*)(char *, int, int, void *)'>,
              <function _pem_password_cb at 0x1077e3488>,
              b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00',
              None),
 4441887216: (<ctype 'int(*)(unsigned char *, int)'>,
              <function _osrandom_rand_bytes at 0x108b6f2f0>,
              b'\xff\xff\xff\xff\x00\x00\x00\x00',
              None),
 4441887248: (<ctype 'int(*)()'>,
              <function _osrandom_rand_status at 0x108b6f378>,
              b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00',
              None),
 '__doc__': None,
 '__loader__': None,
 '__name__': '_cffi_backend._extern_py',
 '__package__': None,
 '__spec__': None}
````

The suspect commit is 830af271de1c9c358, when cryptography was updated to use cffi's static callbacks.  [Here is a sample script that demonstrates the breakage.](https://gist.github.com/felliott/4547366a649dac4df6d1)

I'm relatively new to Python, so I'm not sure if this is a bug in cffi, cryptography, or freezegun. I'm happy to refile elsewhere if needed.  Please let me know if you need any more information.  Thank you very much and have a great day!

Cheers,
Fitz

issue_title: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\U0001f4a9' in position 4: ordinal not in range(128)
issue_body:
With the wifi module enabled, I get an uncaught exception due to a neighbor's weird wifi name.     

The exact exception I get is:
`UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\U0001f4a9' in position 4: ordinal not in range(128)`     

The issue is caused from the following line of code (glances_wifi.py:200)
`msg = '{:{width}}'.format(hotspotname, width=ifname_max_width)`    

The cause being that the default encoding for strings when using format is 'ascii'. This won't be able to handle all characters that are allowed in a ssid (i.e. my neighbors wifi which is: Grey💩On) . 

The code could handle this by replacing unknown characters with '?' or by trying to use UTF-8 instead.

nscanned shows the number of keys or documents scanned.

Simply run queries that are resolved against an index.

issue_title: Inconsistent iterations rate: 30 iterations = 40 seconds?
issue_body:
Hi guys,

Testing very simple bots against each other in realtime mode.

Issues:
1. Iteration 0 is called in the code at the ~1 minute 21 seconds of the game. 
And it is run at different times for each bots. Shouldn't it be run on the first second of the match?

https://i.imgur.com/nuFomNk.png

2. Once some attacking happens between the workers, the iterations stop ticking at regular intervals. And it can take ~40 seconds to count 30 iterations while normally 1 second contains ~30 iterations.

https://i.imgur.com/QWkUSBn.png
https://i.imgur.com/shcsjR7.png

This makes it impossible to code any reasonable bot strategy. For example it takes multiple minutes for defending bot workers to actually attack each of the invading workers. And there are 10-20 seconds gaps between one target is destroyed and the next target attack command is executed.

Have anyone experienced the same?

My Machine: MacOS 10.13.5

Source code:
`import sc2
from sc2 import run_game, maps, Race, Difficulty
from sc2.player import Bot, Computer

class DefendingBot(sc2.BotAI):
    async def on_step(self, iteration):
        if iteration == 0:
            await self.chat_send(f"I am a Defending Bot")
        if (iteration % 30 == 0): # Detection is run every few frames
            await self.chat_send(f"Iteration: {iteration}")
            await self.chat_send(f"Enemies detected: {self.known_enemy_units.amount}")
            if (self.known_enemy_units.amount > 0):
                for worker in self.workers:
                    await self.do(worker.attack(self.known_enemy_units.first))


class InvadingBot(sc2.BotAI):
    async def on_step(self, iteration):
        if iteration == 0: # If this is the first frame
            await self.chat_send(f"I am an Invading Bot")
            for worker in self.workers:
                # Send all workers to the other bot's location
                await self.do(worker.move(self.enemy_start_locations[0]))


run_game(maps.get("AbyssalReefLE"), [
    Bot(Race.Protoss, DefendingBot()),
    Bot(Race.Protoss, InvadingBot()),
], realtime=True)
`

issue_title: FR: AUR packages with GPG signatures
issue_body:
As explained by Allan Mcrae [here](http://allanmcrae.com/2015/01/two-pgp-keyrings-for-package-management-in-arch-linux/), when installing AUR packages which come with a GPG signature, if the user is to avoid importing and locally signing all the relevant GPG keys (from the *package* developers), then something like `validpgpkeys=('F37CDAB708E65EA183FD1AF625EF0A436C2A4AFF')` has to be added to the PKGBUILD. 

Currently, if a package as a GPG sig (e.g. tor-browser-en), makepkg will fail to verify the signature, and thus pkgbuilder will fail to build the package. Could it be possible (and what level of effort would be required) to add a prompt, that allows the user to confirm if a GPG key with the fingerprint in the PKGBUILD is to be regarded as trusted? (And if the answer is 'yes', to add the `validpgpkeys=...` line to the PKGBUILD on the fly).

issue_title: 'Initial' bugs (vs features) break newer release-line-oriented achitecture
issue_body:
## Description

Bugs (explicit or implicit-because-no-issue-role, doesn't matter) listed at the beginning of time before any release entries, break after Releases 1.1.x because the new "derive default version spec for issues" functionality assumes there will always be more than zero line buckets.

This doesn't break for features because their branch in `default_spec()` is more generic, just taking the major number only (which is filled in by the pre-seeking functionality). But the bug branch looks inside the major-version structures and tries to find out what the latest minor release bucket is. When there are none, kaboom:

```
Exception occurred:
  File "/Users/jforcier/Code/oss/releases/releases/models.py", line 88, in default_spec
    key for key, value in six.iteritems(lines)
ValueError: max() arg is an empty sequence
```

## Solutions

* Main issue is conceptual: unless the first release is marked as a bugfix release, a regular, non-major bug has nowhere to go until the first actual bugfix release.
    * This is arguably a sub-problem of #19, which is also the type of changelog I am encountering this bug within.
    * This could just be considered part and parcel of #19 insofar as "early/pre-1.0 releases should/could consider all issues as feature-like".
* Could implement a similar pre-seek/look-ahead here as we do for features, i.e. prefill the lines struct with not only major version but next minor version.
    * Plus: feels sensible/obvious
    * Minus: more complexity; still doesn't help with the conceptual issue of "hm wait I put this bug in my changelog but it's not appearing in my first 0.1.0 release". I.e. fixes the symptom but not the root problem
* Documentation/education only: inform users that if this is a problem for them, they should always stick some initial release before any bugs, *or* ensure pre-first-release items are explicitly marked feature-like in some fashion.
    * This is actually tempting since in my case I am translating a pre-Releases changelog where the first "entry" is simply "First public release" or similar. Put another way, this sort of "pre-release item" only really makes any sense if it's this sort of content-less "START OF TIME LOL" marker. Actual, real *bugs* can't exist before any releases are out.
    * However we should still put a useful error message around the symptomatic lines of code, so at least users know why it's erroring/unhappy.
* Following from thoughts there...perhaps make it so start-of-time situations where the struct looks the way it does here, trigger a "this is an implicit item, make it act feature-like instead of bug-like" behavior.
    * Plus: solves issue neatly, works well with conceptual notes above
    * Minus: means differing behavior at differing parts of the changelog, which isn't amazing.
        * Counterpoint: this is a very special/corner case, and we also already have some differing behavior stuff with the new-in-1.1 pre-major-release stuff, so there is precedent.

issue_title: Feature request: Less strict table parsing
issue_body:
Hey there! 
To get the following table:

Column1 | Column2
------: | :------
   Data | Data
   Data | Data

the Mistletoe parser would require the following code:
```
| Column1 | Column2 |
| ------: | :------ |
|    Data | Data    |
|    Data | Data    |
```

Many Markdown implementations I know allow the following:
```
| Column1 | Column2
| ------: | :------
|    Data | Data
|    Data | Data
```
and in fact, while typing this, I figured out that Github will even parse the following:
```
Column1 | Column2
------: | :------
   Data | Data
   Data | Data
```
I don't see a particular use for the latter, but the first one has often made it much easier for me to maintain visually appealing, ASCII-formatted tables where the last column contains content of very different lengths.

Would it be easy for mistletoe to support this as well? @miyuchina If this is not out of scope but you are busy, I can also try implementing this myself.

issue_title: Not using default configuration paths
issue_body:
Other than stated in the documentation
> By default, ARouteServer looks for this [arouteserver.yml] file in `~/arouteserver` and `/etc/arouteserver/`

ARouteServer raises en error “Configuration file not found”. This is likely because self.cfg is replaced with self.default which has `cfg_dir=None` (pierky/arouteserver/config/program.py:44). Expected behavior is to look for arouteserver.yml at the specified locations.

Workaround is to specify optional parameter `--cfg` and path to arouteserver.yml.

issue_title: Update border_pixels to border_sub_pixels
issue_body: border_pixels is a list of image pixels in the border, used to define the source-plane border. However, we don't necessarily want to trace image pixels to the source-plane, instead only using sub-pixels (and sparse pixels). So, we need a border_sub_pixels, which gives a list of the sub-pixel in each image pixel which is closest to the border.

issue_title: datefinder can't find date with Monday, b'cause all "on" substrings are removed, while dateparser could parse it
issue_body:
Example: date_string= 'On Monday, May 9, 2016'
 _find_and_replace() should remove all DateFinder.REPLACEMENT **words**, but in fact it removes all **substrings**, i.e. within words too, 
so,  _find_and_replace() wrongly mutates this into " mday may 9 2016"
i.e. **it wrongly removes "on" from "monday".**
dateparser can't parse " mday may 9 2016"
but it successfully parses  'On Monday, May 9, 2016'
How to correct: force _find_and_replace() function to not touch substrings within words, at least not touch "monday"s

issue_title: Clarify documentation of a couple things
issue_body:
In `ia upload --help`, it says: 

```
    -R, --retries=<i>                 Number of times to retry request if S3
                                      retruns a 503 SlowDown error.
```

It appears in the code at https://github.com/jjjake/internetarchive/blob/e3c9540942768f6f78eaedbb28e0dce2359128ee/internetarchive/session.py#L115 that the following would be more helpful:
```
    -R, --retries=<i>                 Number of times to retry request if S3
                                      indicates a temporary error.
```

In `ia help`, it says:

```
See 'ia help <command>' for more information on a specific command.
```

The correct format is `ia <command> --help`. The documentation and the application behavior should match.

Thanks! :)

import sys

def update_progress(i, total, length=10, fg="#", bg=" ", decimals=0):

for i in range(0, 100):

```

issue_title: Setting fields to be saved (fields option)
issue_body:
Hello,

I wonder if this setting (along with `exclude`) is working for someone. The problem is `VersionAdapter.get_fields_to_serialize` is a generator. Later this generator is used in `django.core.serializers.base.py`, `serialize` function to check if current field is in a list of fields to serialize. But generators are different than lists/tuples and operator `in` works differently:

```
>>> g = (x for x in range(1, 3)); 1 in g; 2 in g;
True
True

>>> g = (x for x in range(1, 3)); 1 in g; 1 in g;
True
False

>>> g = (x for x in range(1, 3)); 2 in g; 1 in g;
True
False

g = (x for x in range(2, 3)); 1 in g; 2 in g;
False
False
```

So, `VersionAdapter.get_fields_to_serialize` as generator will work only if it'll have all fields and in order `serialize` iterates them.

The solution could be simple: `VersionAdapter.get_serialized_data` function instead of using `get_fields_to_serialize` as generator will convert it to list:

```python
    def get_serialized_data(self, obj):
        """Returns a string of serialized data for the given obj."""
        return serializers.serialize(
            self.get_serialization_format(),
            (obj,),
            fields = list(self.get_fields_to_serialize()), # here we convert generator to a list
        )
```

Or `get_fields_to_serialize` could return a list, not generator. After all, why it returns generator, to save memory?

Am I doing something wrong, or is it library bug?

issue_title: Much faster code for aroon
issue_body:
def aroon_up(data, period):
    return (1 + data.rolling(period).apply(lambda x: pd.Series(x).idxmax(skipna = True))) / period * 100

def aroon_down(data, period):
    return (1 + data.rolling(period).apply(lambda x: pd.Series(x).idxmin(skipna = True))) / period * 100

data must be a pandas.Series object - we could wrap it as pd.Series(data) first.

Current code executes at around 8 seconds on 16285 rows of data.
Code above executes at around 1.36 seconds

issue_title: log lines only shown in 4096 byte intervals
issue_body:
When using `supervisor-stdout`, "docker run" only shows the log lines in 4096 byte intervals.
Only when at least 4096 bytes got logged by the programs, they are output/sent to docker.
The rest of the data is held back/buffered until the next 4096 byte block is full.

Is there a way to configure this? It's hindering me whilst debugging.

My programs had the following supervisor configuration:
```
[eventlistener:stdout]
command = supervisor_stdout
buffer_size = 100
events = PROCESS_LOG
result_handler = supervisor_stdout:event_handler

[program:lalala]
stdout_events_enabled=true
stderr_events_enabled=true
stdout_logfile_maxbytes = 1MB
stdout_logfile_backups = 0
stderr_logfile_maxbytes = 1MB
stderr_logfile_backups = 0
```

----

When not using supervisor-stdout but simply `stdout_logfile=/dev/stdout / redirect_stderr=true / stdout_logfile_maxbytes=0` it works without delay.

----

When starting bash in the docker container, and then manually starting supervisord, the output is immediate.

So I guess it's an issue with docker itself.

issue_title: Handling standard auth-token along with social auth
issue_body:
So far, I have been using the standard Django REST token-auth, while replacing the "Token" keyword by "Bearer" to have the same header as with the django-rest-framework-social-oauth2:

`Authorization: Bearer my_token`

So "my_token" can either be a Token from the auth-token table or an AccessToken returned by auth/convert-view.

To handle both authentications, I thus created the following class:

```
class ExampleAuthentication(TokenAuthentication):

    keyword = 'Bearer'

    def authenticate(self, request):

        token = request.META['HTTP_AUTHORIZATION']
        token = token[len(self.keyword) + 1 :]

        print(token)

        # Django auth-token
        try:
            tok = Token.objects.get(key=token)
            user = User.objects.get(id=tok.user_id)
            return user, None

        except:
            pass

        # social auth by means of DRSA2
        try:
            user = AccessToken.objects.get(token=token).user

            return user, None

        except:
            return None
```

While I manage to get any logged user registered with `auth-token`, when passing an `AccessToken`, to authenticate a social user (using Facebook), I always get `AnonymousUser`

issue_title: Uploading track fails with 401 Unauthorized but still uploads file
issue_body:
I've been using this code to upload to soundcloud for the last 5 months without problem.

```
#!/usr/bin/python2 -u
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import sys
reload( sys )
sys.setdefaultencoding( "utf8" )

import soundcloud

soundfile = sys.argv[1]

print( 'Logging into soundcloud...' )

try:
	client = soundcloud.Client(
		client_id='64c56d14d1844681f7cca8c61ec0082a',
		client_secret='3a793d20fd6cf23da7fd66ccc77a57a1',
		username='username',
		password='password',
	)
except Exception, err:
	print( err )
else:
	print( 'OK' )

	print( 'Uploading ' + soundfile )

	trackdata = {
		'title': soundfile,
		'asset_data': open( soundfile , 'rb' ),
	}

	try:
		track = client.post( '/tracks', track=trackdata )

	else:

```

Since yesterday it fails with the following error:

This error has also been reported by somebody else [on stackoverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/37421764/python-soundcloud-sdk-upload-track)

issue_title: Move User's Folder
issue_body:
Hello,

I'm trying to implement a [Move User's Folder](https://box-content.readme.io/reference#move-folder-into-another-users-folder) from the box content API. I did not see it in the current code base, so please let me know if I missed something. I monkey-patched something like this: 
```
class User(BaseObject):

    def transfer_folder(self, owned_by, folder):
        """
        Moves all of the owned content from within one user’s folder into a new folder in another user’s account.
        :param owned_by: The user who the folder will be transferred to
        :param folder: The folder that will be transferred
        :return:
        """
        url = '{0}/users/{1}/folders/{2}'.format(API.BASE_API_URL, self._object_id, folder.object_id)
        data = { 'owned_by': {'id': owned_by.object_id}}
        #data = { 'owned_by': json.dumps({'id': owned_by.object_id})}
        box_response = self._session.put(url, data=data)
        return box_response.json()
```

I have verified that the url looks right, and based on the rest of the api, the params (and my many variations) seem to be right. Yet I receive as:

I have tried headers, data, and params. How might I format the data correctly?

issue_title: Optimizers memory usage
issue_body:
There's been few comments about memory consumption in the R-package. With large models, the memory footprint can rapidly burst the available memory unless a manual gc() is added in the training loop. 

The problematic appears to be the weights update performed by the optimizers: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/R-package/R/model.R#L221

Memory behaves as if the temporary nd.arrays were kept in memory. 

Looked at how the python optimizers were designed and took a first attempt at using the `mx.nd.sgd.update` type of operators. 
It seemed to help, but looking at https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/python/mxnet/optimizer.py#L434, I confused at how the state of optimizers such as momentum or adam and cie is updated. I can't see where the staste is updated other than for the initialization. 

Also, tried to do a direct mutation of the weight using the out parameters of the `mx.nd.sgd.update` to point to the executor ref.arg.arrays, but it got the following message after a first update: 

```
 Error in mx.nd.sgd.update(weight = weight, out = out, grad = grad, lr = learning.rate,  : 
  ./ndarray.h:87: RCheck failed: ptr_->writable && !ptr_->moved Passing a read only NDArray to mutate function 
```
I'm still missing some pieces to figure out a proper way of fixing the memory consumtion of the optimizers. Any help would be much welcome!

@thirdwing

issue_title: crash when using Concat and binding with grad_req = 'add' in backward()
issue_body:
Hi,

I try to use a Concat layer in my network and run into some errors.

```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
MXNetError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-3-994bb84ede27> in <module>()
     13 
     14 # it crash here
---> 15 executor.backward()
     16 
     17 outputs = dict(zip(symbol.list_outputs(), executor.outputs))

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/mxnet-0.5.0-py2.7.egg/mxnet/executor.pyc in backward(self, out_grads)
    117             self.handle,
    118             mx_uint(len(out_grads)),
--> 119             ndarray))
    120 
    121     def set_monitor_callback(self, callback):

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/mxnet-0.5.0-py2.7.egg/mxnet/base.pyc in check_call(ret)
     74     """
     75     if ret != 0:
---> 76         raise MXNetError(py_str(_LIB.MXGetLastError()))
     77 
     78 def c_str(string):

MXNetError: [11:31:50] src/operator/./concat-inl.h:112: Check failed: (req[i]) == (kWriteTo) 
```

After trying several times, I figure out that it is caused by feeding Variable as (one of) inputs to Concat while binding with grad_req = 'add'.

I use python interface.

The error is raised by src/operator/concat-inl.h#L112 checking the gradient type.

I am not sure why it is checked that way.

When I change to grad_req = 'write' or feeding the output of FC layer as inputs, the error never appears.

I try to repeat the error in a simple way in the following gist: https://gist.github.com/guoquan/959461c8ff2a34fe8ac8

A possible workaround is to split the weight after the concat, but ...

Can anyone help to look into this. Thanks.

issue_title: The DataIter problem with symbol.list_arguments()
issue_body:
In a LSTM bucketing network, we have tow forms of data Iterator to provide the data:
One is  provide the data wrapped together:

``` Python
self.provide_data = [('data', (batch_size, self.default_bucket_key)),
                             ('mask', (batch_size, self.default_bucket_key))] + init_states
self.provide_label = [('softmax_label', (self.batch_size, self.default_bucket_key))]
```

The other is to provide the data at each step:

``` Python
self.provide_data = [('%s/%d' % (self.data_name, t), (self.batch_size,)) for t in range(self.default_bucket_key)] +
        [('%s/%d' % (self.mask_name, k), (self.batch_size,)) for k in range(self.default_bucket_key)] + init_states
self.provide_label = [('%s/%d' % (self.label_name, t), (self.batch_size,)) for t in range(self.default_bucket_key)]
```

And in the fit process, especially at the time we create a DataParallelExecutorGroup:

``` Python
self.aux_names = sym.list_auxiliary_states()
self.param_idx = [i for i in range(len(arg_names)) if arg_names[i] in param_names]
self.param_names = [arg_names[i] for i in self.param_idx]
```

and

``` Python
self.param_arrays = [[e.arg_arrays[i] for e in self.train_execs]
                             for i in self.param_idx]
self.grad_arrays = [[e.grad_arrays[i] for e in self.train_execs]
                            for i in self.param_idx]
```

**Note: The above code is in the executor_manager.py**
We assign some values to the params in the network. However the param_idx is get from the arg_names.
I have following questions:
1. Does symbol.list_arguments has a order? For example, all network parameters followed by the input and output parameters.
2. Does the arg_names change because of the change of bucket in the lstm network?
In my code, it has following error:

```
    epoch_end_callback = [ mx.callback.do_checkpoint( '%s/%s' % (params_dir, expt_name), 100) ]
  File "/home/zhaozijian/mxnet/python/mxnet/model.py", line 785, in fit
    sym_gen=self.sym_gen)
  File "/home/zhaozijian/mxnet/python/mxnet/model.py", line 217, in _train_multi_device
    executor_manager.load_data_batch(data_batch)
  File "/home/zhaozijian/mxnet/python/mxnet/executor_manager.py", line 386, in load_data_batch
    shared_group=self.execgrp)
  File "/home/zhaozijian/mxnet/python/mxnet/executor_manager.py", line 232, in __init__
    for i in self.param_idx]
IndexError: list index out of range
```

And I print the parameters, it seems to have no order:

```
arg_names: ['data/12', 'embed_weight', 'dec_l0_i2h_weight', 'dec_l0_i2h_bias', 'data/11', 'enc_l0_i2h_weight', 'enc_l0_i2h_bias', 'data/10', 'data/9', 'data/8', 'data/7', 'data/6', 'data/5', 'data/4', 'data/3', 'data/2', 'data/1', 'data/0', 'l0_init_h', 'enc_l0_h2h_weight', 'enc_l0_h2h_bias', 'l0_init_c', 'mask/0', 'mask/1', 'mask/2', 'mask/3', 'mask/4', 'mask/5', 'mask/6', 'mask/7', 'mask/8', 'mask/9', 'mask/10', 'mask/11', 'dec_l0_h2h_weight', 'dec_l0_h2h_bias', 'mask/12', 'cls_weight', 'cls_bias', 'label/0', 'data/13', 'mask/13', 'label/1', 'data/14', 'mask/14', 'label/2', 'data/15', 'mask/15', 'label/3', 'data/16', 'mask/16', 'label/4', 'data/17', 'mask/17', 'label/5', 'data/18', 'mask/18', 'label/6', 'data/19', 'mask/19', 'label/7', 'data/20', 'mask/20', 'label/8', 'data/21', 'mask/21', 'label/9', 'data/22', 'mask/22', 'label/10', 'data/23', 'mask/23', 'label/11', 'data/24', 'mask/24', 'label/12']
param_names: ['embed_weight', 'dec_l0_i2h_weight', 'dec_l0_i2h_bias', 'enc_l0_i2h_weight', 'enc_l0_i2h_bias', 'enc_l0_h2h_weight', 'enc_l0_h2h_bias', 'dec_l0_h2h_weight', 'dec_l0_h2h_bias', 'cls_weight', 'cls_bias']
```

I believe the error arises from the change of bucket but I'm not quite sure.
Look forward to your reply.

issue_title: Deadlock during ThreadedEnginePerDevice destructor after CuDNNConvolutionOp<float>::SelectAlgo called.
issue_body:
## Description
I haven't been able to fully track this down, but this is what I've seen so far.  CI is frequently deadlocking after the introduction of the test :  test_operator_gpu:test_op_output_names_monitor in the PR https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/10300

Removing the lines: 
```python
conv_sym = mx.sym.Convolution(data, kernel=(2, 2), num_filter=1, name='conv')
check_name(conv_sym, ['conv_output'])
```
here: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/10300/files#diff-cb652780258e73a9cd08568f38929aa2R5417 will prevent the deadlocks

It appears the test has exposed the deadlock, but that the deadlock was in the codebase prior to this PR.  I believe what is happening is that calling inference on the Conv operator in this way creates two zombie threads that are deadlocked on each other.  The test continues to run (and in fact the full test suite runs) until the ThreadedEnginePerDevice destructor is called, at which point the ThreadPool is brought down, it attempts to join all threads and the process hangs.

It looks like Thread 20 is blocked waiting for this lock: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/operator/nn/cudnn/cudnn_convolution-inl.h#L718 -> https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/engine/threaded_engine.cc#L385

Thread 16 is blocked here: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/operator/custom/custom-inl.h#L121

## Environment info (Required)
CI

## Minimal steps to reproduce
Reproducible locally on a gpu machine with:
```bash
ci/build.py --build --platform ubuntu_build_cuda /work/runtime_functions.sh build_ubuntu_gpu_cuda91_cudnn7
nvidia-docker run -v /home/ubuntu/incubator-mxnet:/work/mxnet -v /home/ubuntu/incubator-mxnet/build:/work/build -u 1000:1000 -ti mxnet/build.ubuntu_gpu bash
```
Then in the container:
```bash
export PYTHONPATH=/work/mxnet/python
export MXNET_STORAGE_FALLBACK_LOG_VERBOSE=0
cd tests/python/gpu/
nosetests-3.4 -v test_operator_gpu:test_op_output_names_monitor
```

issue_title: Support for nullable JSONFields
issue_labels: [{'id': 203192, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyMDMxOTI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-extensions/django-extensions/labels/food%20for%20thought', 'name': 'food for thought', 'color': 'e102d8', 'default': False}, {'id': 203193, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyMDMxOTM=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-extensions/django-extensions/labels/improvement', 'name': 'improvement', 'color': '02d7e1', 'default': False}, {'id': 1003485, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwxMDAzNDg1', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-extensions/django-extensions/labels/need%20help', 'name': 'need help', 'color': '0b02e1', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Currently, adding `null=True` to a `JSONField` is accepted, but silently coerces to `'{}'`. This behaviour is both unexpected, and can impact the performance of migrations (which might be performed during a site release, therefore causing downtime).

As a user, I would expect that if nullable JSON fields are not supported, that an exception would be raised when instantiating with `null=True`.

If nullable fields are supposed to be supported, I would expect to be able to filter querysets with `__isnull=True`, and I would expect that passing `null=True` would result in a null field being added to the table in the database. In the case of Postgres, null fields are "free" to add to a table, so this is desirable with large tables.

issue_title: Bug: Max 100 activities retrieved from account
issue_body:
There is a bug when calling the `get_activies` function from the account object: the max number of retrieved activities is always 100 irrespective of the time range. 


Python API v2.9.1:

```python
from facebookads.adobjects.adaccount import AdAccount
account = AdAccount(account_id)
res= account.get_activities(params={'fields' ['actor_name,extra_data,event_time,object_name,translated_event_type,application_name']
                              })
print(len(res)) #will print 100
```
However, when using the normal rest API (paginating the results) I can get all the activities, which hints that this is a bug of the way this python API does pagination.

REST API:

```python
path = 'https://graph.facebook.com/v2.8/{}/activities?fields=actor_name,extra_data,event_time,object_name,translated_event_type,application_name&access_token={}'.format(account_id, token)
result = list()

while True:

    r= requests.get(path)
    r= r.json()
    
    result.extend(r['data'])

    if 'paging' in r and 'next' in r['paging']:
        path=r['paging']['next']
    else:
        break
print(len(result)) # Will print 256 for the account I am running this test on
```

This bug appears with all accounts which have more than 100 activities in the default time range.

issue_title: Use gevent to respond to messages asynchronously
The order of query results is wrong when ORDER BY is used
For scenario calculations, check that num of realizations equals to num of distinct GMPEs
We need an event based risk calculator starting from the GMFs
issue_body:
In the past most (virtually all) risk calculations running in a cluster where dominated by the risk part, thus it made sense to recompute the GMFs on-the-fly every time. That had the advantage of reducing the data transfer significantly. For instance for Colombia the GMFs are 100 GB, the ruptures, 0.5 GB, so the data transfer is reduced by 200 times by computing the GMFs on the fly.

However things are different now. We introduced recently the `minimum_intensity` feature and that changes the rules of the game. It is now possible to filter away the GMFs below the minimum intensity, and that means that typically 99% of the GMFs can be removed (in the case of Colombia with minimum_intensity PGA=0.1 the 100 GB becomes 0.9 GB). This kind of reduction is typical for our calculations. So it makes sense to compute the GMFs once and to store them since after the filtering they are few and easy to transfer.

Also, now the GMFs are stored in the HDF5 format in an ultra-efficient way (and they will be stored in an even more efficient way in the future, using a new feature of h5py ) so it is not an issue to store even terabytes of them. A storing speed of 500+ MB/s has been measured on Wilson. Transferring hundreds of the gigabytes is achievable too, if the transfer is done carefully, i.e. if you don't try to transfer everything at once.

Due to those advances, saving the GMFs has become reasonable now. Having the GMFs on disk allow different parallelization strategies for the risk that will result in a big speedup. Moreover computing the GMFs in the risk part has a big memory cost, whereas computing them in the hazard part has a much smaller memory cost due to the differences in the algorithm used.

Having a GMF-based calculator may also be a necessary preliminary step for the UCERF calculation, since it is too large to be done with the current calculator (it will likely run out of memory).

Another important use case is the case of a workstation/laptop: when there are few cores, the hazard part is typically dominating the risk calculation, so it makes sense to cache the GMFs to support non-cluster situations.

NB: the current calculator based on the rupture has to be maintained because there are still computations dominated by the risk part and where it makes sense to recompute the GMFs on the fly.
The user will be able to select the old calculator or the new one by specifying the calculation_mode in the job.ini file. `event_based_risk` or `ebr_from_gmf`.

PS: having a calculator starting from the GMFs is needed for two other reasons, apart from performance:
1. it makes it easier to debug and test the calculator
2. a third party can give us the GMFs and we can start the risk from there.

issue_title: create_submodule with branch 'master' gets error on submodule update
issue_body:
Not sure if this is a real issue, or a failure in my understanding, but anyway:

In an automation script, I clone a GitHub repo,
                 
    repo = git.Repo.clone_from (repo_url, repo_path, branch='master')

and `create_submodule` with the branch set to 'master'

    repo.create_submodule (name=submodule_path, path=submodule_path, submodule_url=url, branch='master', no_checkout=True)

This succeeds ok, although what is written to the .gitmodules is **branch = origin/refs/heads/master**
The submodule_path is 2 levels deep, like e.g.  'imports/module_a' or similar
I commit & push that change.

On my dev machine I clone the repo with the newly added submodule:

    git clone <url> workspace
    cd workspace
    git submodule init
    git submodule update --remote

The last command gets an error:

    fatal: needed a single revision
    unable to find current origin/refs/heads/master revision in submodule path 'path/to/submodule'

If I do it all manually using:

    git submodule add -b master <url> <path>

Then what gets written to .gitmodules is **branch = master** and on the submodule update there is no error, and master updates fine.

issue_title: Executable File Format, Yes or No?
issue_body:
There have been a number of issues/comments that tend to trace back to a single question.. Should ``Pipfile`` be executable or not?

The relevant comments are:

> @defnull said in https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/issues/8#issue-190673838
> Parsing and editing ``Pipfile`` on the other hand is significantly more complicated that ``requirements.txt``. IDEs will most likely not support ``Pipfile`` editing for a long time.

> @jayfk said in https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/issues/8#issuecomment-261959575
> On top of that, please don't forget server side tools working with dependencies (pyup.io, requires.io, etc.). There's no way to support ``Pipfile``s if they allow to run arbitrary Python code. Local developer tools might have a chance to establish a working solution over time, server side tools don't.

> @defnull said in https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/issues/9#issue-190734454
> Build or install tools (pip) cannot check if ``Pipfile`` and ``Pipfile.lock`` are out of sync without executing the Pipfile (which is bad, see #7) and cannot warn the user.

> @defnull said in https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/issues/7#issue-190667376
> Executable build descriptions are a bad idea. See https://www.reddit.com/r/Python/comments/5e2vci/pipfile_a_new_and_much_better_way_to_declare/da9c2ku/ or uncountable blog posts and articles all over the Internet for very good examples. It should be common knowledge by now, still the same errors are made again and again.
>
> Pipfile has a nice hybrid approach in that it allows developers to generate a static ``Pipfile.lock`` from a dynamic description. Tools can parse the static ``Pipfile.lock`` and know exactly what to do, while developers can work with the more convenient ``Pipfile`` and can be as smart or lazy as they want to. This approach might actually work very well.
> For this to work, and for others like me to accept this idea, I thing the following point should be stressed out more: Tools work with ``Pipfile.lock`` exclusively. A build system should never automatically execute a ``Pipfile`` to generate a missing or outdated ``Pipfile.lock``. The ``pipfile`` module should never be a build requirement.

> @takluyver said in https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/issues/6#issuecomment-261931518
> I don't like specifying metadata in executable files in general. #8 gives one reason why not - it's very hard to reliably modify scripts programmatically.

There's also some reaction on https://www.reddit.com/r/Python/comments/5e2vci/pipfile_a_new_and_much_better_way_to_declare/

issue_title: Round to int, not float
issue_body:
@astrofrog added bounding box methods that call `round`:
https://github.com/astropy/regions/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=round

Now here's what I think is an amazing catch by PyCharm (or a false positive if I misunderstand the situation): It points out that e.g. `CirclePixelRegion.bounding_box` calls `BoundingBox(ixmin, ixmax, iymin, iymax)` with floats, but it should call it with ints.

Now first of all, I think it knows about the expected types because it's parsing the `BoundingBox` Numpy-format docstring. Also, it asked me if this project should be simultaneously Python 2.7 and 3 compatible and I said yes. And lastly, on Python 2, `round` returns float:
```
$ python2
>>> type(round(0.3))
<type 'float'>

$ python3
>>> type(round(0.3))
<class 'int'>
```

@astrofrog @adonath - Is this indeed a potential issue? I think we should change callers to always call `int`, instead of auto-casting of ints stored in `float` in `BoundingBox.__init__`? Should we add an extra check that ints are passed in? What's the best (correct, short) way to write this, i.e. what code changes should we make?

Should we do a general review of `round` code in `astropy`, `photutils`, `gammapy` or just make a change here? Does one of you have time to make a PR or should I?

issue_title: Feature request - masks from apertures
issue_body:
This may be better thought of as a pyregion issue, but it is relevant to the existing photutils setup and highlights some of the overlap between the two packages.

I would like to be able to return a 2D mask from an image given an input aperture (either Sky or pixel).  Both a boolean mask (containing all overlap or all contained pixels) and a floating-point fractional pixel mask could be useful depending on the situation.

This is also related to #161, but specifically about 2D masks.

issue_title: Reasonable box_size for Background2D?
issue_body:
The [2D Background and Noise Estimation](https://photutils.readthedocs.io/en/stable/background.html#d-background-and-noise-estimation) states that:

>  Background2D requires the size of the box (`box_size`) in which to estimate the background. Selecting the box size requires some care by the user. The box size should generally be larger than the typical size of sources in the image, but small enough to encapsulate any background variations.

All other parameters have some reasonable default values that can be used, but not `box_size`.

Is there a reasonable rule of thumb for selecting this value? I've found that (for some frames I'm processing) a value of ~7xFWHM produces reasonably cleaned images. Smaller values produce a noisy cleaned image (`data - bckg`) and larger values show no improvements.

Is this a reasonable rule or it's this the case for my images only? Can a reasonable general rule even be stated? If so, could it be added to the docs.

issue_title: Projection of apertures along a dimension: photutils->3D spectrum extraction
issue_body:
This is a feature request for IFU and Spectral Cube purposes.  A wrapper to extract photometry from each channel in a cube (and preserve the relevant WCS information) would be very helpful.

For radio cubes, this is generally trivial (i.e., photutils probably isn't even needed), but for IFUs you often want to weight by the PSF to do "optimal" spectral extraction for stars.

issue_title: Solve stdc++ ABI incompatibility between manylinux nupic.bindings and recent Linux distros
issue_body:
It turns out that the c\+\+11 standard prescribed a new ABI for certain classes, but compiler toolchains took their own sweet time to implement that ABI. While they support -std=c\+\+11, the implementation of the new ABI is not uniform. The baseline C++ toolchain prescribed by the manylinux policy supports std=c\+\+11, but doesn’t implement the new ABI. The 5.4.0 toolchain on Ubuntu 16.04 implements the new ABI, which alters declarations of some standard classes, and exceptions in particular.

One area where this impacts us is in the ability to catch some exceptions. For example, https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/b9247a270e2fae2f4586286163b6472a909560e2/src/nupic/regions/VectorFile.cpp#L177 catches ios_base::failure&. This is one of the exceptions impacted by the ABI change. In the manylinux toolchain, it’s simply derived from std::exception, which is the signature that gets compiled into nupic.core when building a manylinux wheel. However, the new ABI mandates that it should be derived from std::system_error (and sets some additional attributes), which gives it a different signature. Thus, our manylinux build is unable to catch that exception when running on a system whose stdc\+\+ library incorporates the new ABI (e.g. Ubuntu 16.04). There is a discussion about it in https://gcc.gnu.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=66145.

For this specific case, the workaround may be to catch std::exception instead of or in addition to ios_base::failure or to restructure the use of streaming API to not make use of exceptions. However, the vast body of 3rd party code in nupic.core also makes use of this specific exception,  and the ABI change may impact some other classes, likely making it impractical to make such adaptations in the overall vast code base.

Another possibility that may be more practical:
1. Include libstdc++ statically
2. And use a version script in the builds of the nupic.bindings extension shared libs that hides all symbols except the extension initialization functions.
 * When libstdc\+\+ links statically, it's symbol references still show up as public in the generated extension shared libs. This means that if something else in the application (e.g., pycapnp) causes the shared libstdc\+\+ to be loaded, then the C\+\+ references in our extensions might be linked to the shared libstdc\+\+ that implements the new ABI instead of the statically-linked libstdc\+\+.

issue_title: Switch from Last-Modified to ETag headers?
issue_body:
I think your `Last-Modified` headers are violating [RFC7232](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7232#section-2.2). According to this RFC, you need to use that long human-readable date format, chosen in a time when 1000 milliseconds was still a reasonable versioning granularity. :)

This means compliant http clients would consider your `Last-Modified` headers to be malformed, possibly leading to unpredictable behavior.

It is also common for a browser or other http client (or any proxy or CDN along the way) to insert its own `If-Modified-Since` header whenever it has a version of the document in cache, and there is no telling how a server implementation would react to that.

Also, violating RFCs is naughty. ;)

GoogleDrive, Dropbox, ownCloud-flavoured WebDAV, CouchDB, and remoteStorage all use ETag instead of Last-Modified. We used to have Last-Modified millisecond-timestamps in the remoteStorage protocol as well, but switched to ETags, because they do effectively the same thing, but are treated as opaque strings by http clients.

To make requests fail if a pre-existing resource changed:
* `If-Unmodified-Since: 1234567890123` -> `If-Match: "1234567890123"`

To make requests fail if a pre-existing resource did not change:
* `If-Modified-Since: 1234567890123` -> `If-None-Match: "1234567890123"`

To make requests fail if a presumed-absent resource exists:
* `If-Modified-Since: 0` -> `If-None-Match: *`

In http responses:
* `Last-Modified: 1234567890123` -> `ETag: "1234567890123"`

The downsides:
* Even when retrieving a resource for the first time, `If-Modified-Since` headers can be used to only retrieve a resource if it is recent, and `If-Unmodified-Since` to only retrieve it if it has stabilized. When using ETags, a client would have to do a filtered query on the collection first, and then decide whether to retrieve the resource or not. So you may have an extra roundtrip there, but I think this is not a very common case.
*  If you know the `Last-Modified` timestamp of a collection, then you could in theory forget the modification times of individual documents, and save some representation space. With ETags, the client needs to remember the ETag of each resource as metadata. But I think most client implementations would already do that anyway.

issue_title: bibedit: templates ignore indicators on expansion
issue_labels: [{'id': 96304291, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Branch:%20maint-1.1', 'name': 'Branch: maint-1.1', 'color': 'd4c5f9', 'default': False}, {'id': 96241276, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Component:%20BibEdit', 'name': 'Component: BibEdit', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False}, {'id': 96237777, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Type:%20bug', 'name': 'Type: bug', 'color': 'ff2a00', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
It seems that upon expansion of `bibedit` record templates the indicators set get ignored:

```xml
  <datafield tag="856" ind1="4" ind2=" ">
    <subfield code="u">VOLATILE:URL</subfield>
    <subfield code="y">VOLATILE:Table of Contents, Inhaltsverzeichnis,  full text, Download</subfield>
  </datafield>
```

exposes `$u` and `$y` as subfields in `8560_` instead of adding a new `8564_` field.

As `websubmit` documents always add a `8560_f`, the templates suggests to add `$u` and `$y` to this very field.

issue_title: Supporting local output formats
issue_body:
Originally on 2010-11-04

When an updated version of Invenio is reinstalled over an already existing installation, any customization made to invenio-local.conf, any webstyle, any new websubmit function, any new bibformat element, any new format template are preserved. 

Output formats, however, have their names hardcoded into Invenio codebase, and the administrator will usually customize them in place, instead of creating new files. Hence, when make install is performed, any customization is lost.

To support -local suffix ala invenio(-local).conf might be a nice feature to have, so that administrators can simply customize a default output format (say HD.bfo can be customized by the admin by creating a file called HD-local.bfo which would not be overwritten by make install)

```
 curl -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"token":"eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJvcmlnX2lhdCI6MTUwMDI5OTUyNCwidXNlcl9pZCI6IlMxNjQxNzMiLCJleHAiOjE1MDAzODU5MjR9.mTNn44Xm9lBp1v8WkUrkp3kdYSCsOrqHACa7fVq2qi8"}' http://localhost:8000/api-token-verify/
 {"non_field_errors":["Invalid payload."]}%
```

I did a print of my payload and here is what I get
```
{'orig_iat': 1500299524, 'user_id': u'S164173', 'exp': 1500385924}
eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJvcmlnX2lhdCI6MTUwMDI5OTUyNCwidXNlcl9pZCI6IlMxNjQxNzMiLCJleHAiOjE1MDAzODU5MjR9.mTNn44Xm9lBp1v8WkUrkp3kdYSCsOrqHACa7fVq2qi8
```


I am not using django's default auth mechanism, my auth works like this,  i pass in a user id to my api endpoint, it verifies it against an external db, and then sends an otp to the client after the verification of the otp i will return the jwt token to authenticate with other api endpoints.


```
 payload = jwt_payload_handler(client_id)
 token = jwt_encode_handler(payload)
if (result[0] == otp):
                payload = jwt_payload_handler(client_id)
                access_token = jwt_encode_handler(payload)
                print(payload)
                print(access_token)
                return Response({'message': 'Partner Verified',
                                'access_token': access_token}, 
                                status=status.HTTP_200_OK)
```


Here is the custom function i implemented based on what was suggested over here https://github.com/GetBlimp/django-rest-framework-jwt/issues/145


```
from datetime import datetime
from calendar import timegm
from rest_framework_jwt.settings import api_settings

def jwt_payload_handler(client_id):
    """ Custom payload handler
    Token encrypts the dictionary returned by this function, and can be decoded by rest_framework_jwt.utils.jwt_decode_handler
    """
    return {
        'user_id': client_id,
        'exp': datetime.utcnow() + api_settings.JWT_EXPIRATION_DELTA,
        'orig_iat': timegm(
            datetime.utcnow().utctimetuple()
        )
    }

def jwt_response_payload_handler(token, user=None, request=None):
    """ Custom response payload handler.

    This function controlls the custom payload after login or token refresh. This data is returned through the web API.
    """
    return {
        'token': token
    }
```
Can someone please point out what is happening here and what can i do to make it work, How can i override the default authentication so that it works without the user model in place and it validates against (say a client_id which exists in my external remote database) ?

issue_title: Add documentation on Wiki for developers
issue_body:
`numexpr` is a great project, and in order for it to grow in 2016+, it will require PRs from other developers, since the original authors are busy (but thankfully still reviewing accepting PRs).

I would like to help add functionality to `numexpr`, and at this point it is very challenging to get up to speed.  For example, if I wanted to add `nansum`, the best thing to do right now is to find a commit where similar functionality was added, study it, and try to replicate the changes.

A simple guide to adding functions would be immediately useful.  In the short term, there are two kinds of functions that people want to add:

1. element wise
1. reducers (like sum across axis)

If they had a template on how to go about adding these, we would see more PRs.  Hopefully, it would reduce issues like https://github.com/pydata/numexpr/issues/163, https://github.com/pydata/numexpr/issues/87, https://github.com/pydata/numexpr/issues/120 .

Longer term, it would be good to get a sense of how the compiler works, and what to do if one wanted to extend it with new types,  or other functionality.  E.g. it would be really cool if I could use numba to write a n element-wise function, and then pass it to numexpr (which would automagically create an opcode, and use it). 

Bottom line is that if people think they can add to the project with 4 hours or work (say a Saturday afternoon) or less of effort, they're more likely to do so.  By providing some documentation, the maintainers of this project can empower others to help contribute.

issue_title: crash inside brotli::BrotliCompressor::WriteBrotliData()
issue_body:
## Abstract 
When doing compression, using the WriteBrotliData() 3-times in a row, the program will crash.

Hint:
the argument for 'is_last' is always 'true'. Setting this argument to false, will "fix" this crash.

### Expected behavior
Even if I use is_last=true multiple times, I expect no crashes.
Instances of BrotliCompressor should maintain a "safe" internal state
and should not crash.

## Example program, which crashes:
https://gist.github.com/nitram509/081b5826e173910eba20097cba9dde8c

## Environment
The program crashes on these environments
* Windows 10, 64bit, MinGW-w64 v3.4, GCC v4.9.2
* Windows 10, 64bit, Visual C++ 2015 Update v2
* Ubuntu Linux, 64bit, GCC v4.9.2-10

The program will NOT crash on OSX, even when doing 100x WriteBrotliData().
* OSX (latest), 64bit, LLVM v7.3.0, clang-703.0.31

## Background information
I'm the author of jbrotli (Java bindings for (lib-)brotli).
A user of jbrotli filled an issue and it seems to me, there is a bug inside brotli itself.
So I wrote a minimal C-program to demonstrate, what's going on inside the Java-program.

issue_title: Segmentation fault when running Flask development server in Alpine Linux
issue_body:
Hi,

I am getting a segmentation fault when using Raven with the Flask development server on Alpine Linux. Oddly, this issue only appears to occur when I install a recent version of the `requests` library (>= v2.13.0) (even if I don't import that library in my code). This issue is only specific to Alpine - it works fine on other Python Docker images. The issue occurs on versions of Raven >= 5.4.0.

I think the segmentation fault is related to some imports within the Raven library.
When I change the following lines it fixes the problem:
https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/conf/remote.py#L18-L19

from:

```py
from raven.transport.threaded import ThreadedHTTPTransport
from raven.transport.http import HTTPTransport
```

to:

```py
from raven.transport import ThreadedHTTPTransport
from raven.transport import HTTPTransport
```

i.e. remove the submodule, which isn't needed since in `transport/__init__.py` there is:

```py
from raven.transport.http import *  # NOQA
```

I have created a test project to reproduce this issue:
https://github.com/ingresso-group/flask-raven-debug

**Environment:**
- `python:3.6-alpine` Docker image
- Python 3.6.1
- Flask 0.12
- Werkzeug 0.12.1
- Raven 6.0.0

issue_title: small config bug
issue_body:
There's a line in galsim/config/noise.py that does this:
```
cn -= galsim.UncorrelatedNoise(rng, im.wcs, current_var)
```

However, the call sequence for galsim.UncorrelatedNoise requires the arguments to be in the order variance, rng, scale, wcs.  So it's breaking with a TypeError as it tries to use the supplied rng as a noise variance.

The fix for this is obvious and I can do it on a branch (+look for other places where UncorrelatedNoise and CorrelatedNoise might be called incorrectly, but I think this might be it).  The harder question is can we design a unit test that would catch this kind of thing?  It's hard to come up with a really fast one, but I'm happy to get your ideas.

issue_title: Not able to use pip-accel both globally and inside a virtual environment
issue_body:
I've installed pip-accel globally, but when I try to use it inside a virtual environment I get this error:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pip_accel/cli.py", line 56, in main
    accelerator = PipAccelerator(config)
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pip_accel/__init__.py", line 109, in __init__
    self.validate_environment()
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pip_accel/__init__.py", line 141, in validate_environment
    """, environment=environment, prefix=sys.prefix)
EnvironmentMismatchError: You are trying to install packages in environment #1 which is different from environment #2 where pip-accel is installed! Please install pip-accel under environment #1 to install packages there.
```
This is caused by [validate_environment](https://github.com/paylogic/pip-accel/blob/master/pip_accel/__init__.py#L118), which doesn't make much sense. Since `pip-accel` uses `pip` under the hood, why should I have to install another version of `pip-accel` inside every virtual environment? The kicker is that if `pip-accel` is already installed globally, `pip` won't even let you install it inside a virtual environment. It's either one or the other.

issue_title: Consider bravado-core to manage OpenAPI/Swagger 2.0 validation/parser
issue_body:
I have found that we are dealing a lot with OpenAPI/Swagger 2.0 Specification validation and parsing in Connexion code. I was willing to implement an library independent of Connexion code that would manage all those reference checks and validations in a way that could be used by Connexion and other packages that want to deal with OpenAPI Specification. I have found that there is already a package that does exactly what I was looking for. It is called [bravado-core](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core) by Yelp. 

The question is, have you already considered that before? If so, why not reuse that code? If not, what do you think about that idea?

There are many advantages and disadvantages of going that way. Like the core functionality of Connexion being dependent of an external library being the biggest concern. But there are also great benefits like a lot of work is done ahead on supporting the OpenAPI specification.

issue_title: Support for catkin_install_python
issue_body:
Per http://docs.ros.org/kinetic/api/catkin/html/howto/format2/installing_python.html, one should use `catkin_install_python` to install python scripts. However, catkin_lint does not recognize this command and consequently warns about uninstalled files.

Example CMakeLists.txt:

```
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 2.8.3)
project(my_test_project)

find_package(catkin REQUIRED)

catkin_package(
	CATKIN_DEPENDS
	DEPENDS
)

install(
	DIRECTORY launch
	DESTINATION
		${CATKIN_PACKAGE_SHARE_DESTINATION}
)

catkin_install_python(PROGRAMS
	scripts/${PROJECT_NAME}
	DESTINATION ${CATKIN_PACKAGE_SHARE_DESTINATION}/scripts
)
```

where `catkin lint -W2` complains about `my_test_project: warning: executable file 'scripts/my_test_project' is not installed` even though it is.

issue_title: Gates not collected in Sigmet files not properly marked.
issue_body: When reading a Sigmet file with Py-ART, if the number of bins (gates) changes within a sweep, rays which have few gates than the first ray in the sweep will not be properly masked.  Specifically for the "short" rays all gates beyond the last collected gate will have a value corresponding to the field specific conversion of a raw value of 1.  For 2-byte velocities field this is -327.67 which can be seen in XSAPR RHI files (for example the file XNW110524015547.RAW7KB6).  The number of valid gates in each ray is recorded in the "nbins" key in the metadata returned by the SigmetFile.read_data() method which is extracted from the ray headers.  This parameter should be used to mask gates in each ray which were not collected either in the **read_sigmet** function or more likely in the **SigmetFile** class itself.

A build (https://travis-ci.org/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/builds/100902378) that began shortly after another (https://travis-ci.org/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/builds/100902340) gave a false negative due to the other test creating a topic:

```
test_message_pull_mode_e2e
    self.assertFalse(topic.exists())
```

----

While we're at it, we should probably use a custom namespace in datastore tests.

issue_title: Add support for Google Cloud Bigtable in SQLAlchemy
issue_body:
It would be very useful to have Google Bigtable support in Python SQLAlchemy.

It seems Cloud MySQL is fine since SQL Alchemy supports it. However, for NoSQL tables, it seems there is not yet support from Google Bigtable.

Also, Google Big Query should have  compliant interface with SQL Alchemy.
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues/2434


It will really help to develop new applications from Bigtable.

issue_body:
Dependent on #1921, we need to add a SIGTERM handler that can publish thread trace and heap info to Error Reporting.

Example interface:

```
from google.cloud import error_reporting
client = error_reporting.Client(project='my-project',
                                               credentials=creds,
                                               shutdownLogs={threadDump: True, heapInfo: False})
```
