issue_title: Datastore: receiving duplicate entities during query iteration
issue_body:
Hi.

I'm using google-cloud-datastore==1.3.0 from pypi.

When iterating over a query iterator the google-cloud-datastore sometimes returns the same entitiy twice.

I've had a look at the source and it seems that the last entity of a fetched page is the same as the first entity of the next page.

Iterating of pages and printing each enitity id makes the problem obvious.
```python
query_iter = qry.fetch()
pages = query_iter.pages
while True:
    page = next(pages)
    if not page:
        return

    for ent in page:
        print(ent.key.id)

    print("-- new page --")
```

The output looks like this:
```
6438744212111360
5774037759295488
5226622299930624
6390563805855744
5537056630505472
5007404484788224
5289011263307776
5805078561685504
-- new page --
5805078561685504
4785039263924224
4655690686660608
5253444773347328
5481464721309696
```

As you can see the entity 5805078561685504 is returned twice.

Dumping the protocol buffer shows that the 'cursor'-value of the last entity and is identical to the 'end_cursor'-value. Here are the last lines of the dump:```

Maybe you can help.

issue_title: cfn2py generates "from troposphere.lambda import Function"
issue_body:
This import results in a syntax error (clash with Python lambda keyword).

With issue #268 the troposphere lambda module was renamed to awslambda to avoid this clash.
Hence, shouldn't cfn2py generate "from troposphere.awslambda import Function"?

issue_title: How to create a wheel file?
issue_body:
Hi all,

I'm trying to figure out how to create a wheel file based on the code. I already have a target that creates a `tar.gz` file:

```python
# Generate an sdist
# has to be separate from the actual target that collects source files (in this case :src)
# because otherwise it'll error out with a cryptic exception
python_library(
  name="sdist",
  dependencies=[
    ":src",
  ],
  provides=setup_py(
    name="barbequeue",
  ...
  )
)
```

and then running `/pants setup-py :sdist` creates a source dist inside the `dist` folder.

Now I'm trying to create a `whl` file, and looks like the option `--setup-py-run` is the ticket. Unfortunately, running:
```bash
./pants setup-py --setup-py-run="bdist_wheel" :sdist
```

returns an error:
```
arbequeue/dist/barbequeue-0.0.1**** Failed to install barbequeue-0.0.1 (caused by: NonZeroExit("received exit code 1 during execution of `[u'/usr/bin/python', '-', 'bdist_wheel']` while trying to execute `[u'/usr/bin/python', '-', 'bdist_wheel']`",)
):
stdout:

stderr:
usage: setup.py [global_opts] cmd1 [cmd1_opts] [cmd2 [cmd2_opts] ...]
   or: setup.py --help [cmd1 cmd2 ...]
   or: setup.py --help-commands
   or: setup.py cmd --help

error: invalid command 'bdist_wheel'
```

I've tried to make the `wheel` package a dependency of the `:sdist` target, to no avail. Anything I can do here? Do I need to add another dependency, or mess with the current `setuptools`?

issue_title: Documentation issue with `--pants-config-files`
issue_body:
In the docs there's a section saying
>Use the --pants-config-files command-line option to specify a second .ini file. Each of this .ini file's values override the corresponding value in pants.ini, if any.

This isn't how the option works. Here's a pretty easy to reproduce example...

```jim$ ./pants  --pants-config-files='["pants-jenkins.ini","pants.ini"]' options | grep cache | grep write_to
cache.write_to = ['https://myhost.com:8080/pants', '/Users/jim/pantscache'] (from CONFIG)

jim$ ./pants  --pants-config-files='["pants.ini", "pants-jenkins.ini"]' options | grep cache | grep write_to
cache.write_to = ['/Users/jim/pantscache'] (from CONFIG)

jim$ ./pants  --pants-config-files='["pants-jenkins.ini"]' options | grep cache | grep write_to
cache.write_to = ['https://myhost.com:8080/pants', '/Users/jim/pantscache'] (from CONFIG)

jim$ ./pants  --pants-config-files='["pants.ini"]' options | grep cache | grep write_to
cache.write_to = ['/Users/jim/pantscache'] (from CONFIG)

jim$ ./pants  --pants-config-files='pants.ini' options | grep cache | grep write_to
cache.write_to = ['/Users/jim/pantscache'] (from CONFIG)

jim$ ./pants  --pants-config-files='pants-jenkins.ini' options | grep cache | grep write_to
cache.write_to = ['/Users/jim/pantscache'] (from CONFIG)
```

issue_title: The Checkstyle task should use a shaded checkstyle tool
issue_body:
Some checkstyle checks require classloading of user classpaths and since Checkstyle depends on several common / popular 3rdparty libraries, conflicts can easily arise.

@mateor ran into this recently hitting a conflict between the checkstyle 5.6 dep on the old google-collections lib and use code depending on the modern guava lib (both contain overlapping packages).

issue_title: Stream BatchSize property ignored in SAM template
issue_body:
Hi,
it seems that  BatchSize specified in SAM template for DynamoDB streams is ignored.

```
LambdaFunction:
      ......
      Events:
        DynamoDBNewItemEvent:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Stream:
              "Fn::GetAtt": [ "TableFileImport", "StreamArn" ]
            StartingPosition: "LATEST"
            BatchSize: 10
```

I have compared the original template with processed one in CloudFormation and there is no BatchSize properties in the latter.
The stream gets default BatchSize=100

The similar issue was posted on AWS Forum but for Kinesis Streams [See there](https://forums.aws.amazon.com/thread.jspa?messageID=757522&#757522)

issue_title: A few suggestions concerning binary downloads
issue_body:
I installed imageio 3-4 times, and once "import imageio" produced a bug because it could not download a binary (temporary connection problem I guess). Just running the script a second time worked fine.

So maybe it would be worth combining the try/except statement with a "for" loop to retry binary downloads several times when it doesn't work. This way you're quasi-sure that it will always work, and when you get a bug report saying it couldn't download the binaries, you're sure it's something serious.

Also I think the binary downloads could use more text, like (maybe exagerated)

    [imageio] X.exe wasn't found on your computer, downloading it now. This will only be done once.

And in case of bug: "Downlaod failed, please report the issue or try again in a moment".

Cheers !

This breaks automated deployment, specifically, it stops us from adding `gulp scripts` to [`manage.py update`](https://github.com/onespacemedia/server-management/blob/master/server_management/management/commands/update.py), forcing us to run `manage.py update`, then ssh to the server and run `gulp scripts` interactively, kill it, then run `collectstatic`.

Usually for UI / UX we would have wireframes so that we can map exactly what is being delivered to what is expected. For this command line tool the following is expected:

    dlcli <apikey> import <directory name>   # imports all files

    dlcli <apikey> export   # exports everything

    dlcli <apikey> --import-plugin <plugin name>   # imports a specific plugin

    dlcli <apikey> --export-plugin <plugin name>   # exports a specific plugin

    dlcli <apikey> --import-dashboard <dashboard name>   # imports a specific dashboard

    dlcli <apikey> --export-dashboard <dashboard name>   # exports a specific dashboard

    dlcli <apikey> --import-rule <rule name>   # imports a specific rule

    dlcli <apikey> --export-rule <rule name>   # exports a specific rule

    dlcli <apikey> --import-plugin <plugin name> --import-dashboard <dashboard name> --import-rule <rule name>

In all cases prompt for overwrite y / n.

## YAML Wireframe

elasticsearch.yaml:

    Name: elasticsearch
    Type: dashboard
    Period: 1 hour
    Widgets:
        - Title: Active Shards
            Column: 1
            Row: 1
            Size X: 1
            Size Y: 1

issue_title: python 2 support
Issue with dirichlet prior for bayesian learning
issue_body:
The issue is with Bayesian Estimator.
http://pgmpy.org/estimators.html#bayesian-estimator
 The prior type can be one of the following: ‘dirichlet’, ‘BDeu’, ‘K2’
My issue is with the dirichlet, and the parameter: "pseudo_counts". It seems like pgmpy only allows for ONE set of pseudo counts-(a0...ak) where k is the range of states for the target variable (the variable in the rows of the cpd). However this is NOT the accepted definition for the dirichlet pseudo counts-there should be a different set of pseudo counts (a0..ak) for every different combination of the targets variable's parents(for every column in the cpd)

For example-in the example you wrote in the documentation:
You only allow one set of pseudo counts(1,2) for each and every one of the columns(combination of C's parents)
╒══════╤══════╤══════╤══════╤════════════════════╕
│ A    │ A(0) │ A(0) │ A(1) │ A(1)               │
├──────┼──────┼──────┼──────┼────────────────────┤
│ B    │ B(0) │ B(1) │ B(0) │ B(1)               │
├──────┼──────┼──────┼──────┼────────────────────┤
│ C(0) │ 0.25 │ 0.25 │ 0.5  │ 0.3333333333333333 │
├──────┼──────┼──────┼──────┼────────────────────┤
│ C(1) │ 0.75 │ 0.75 │ 0.5  │ 0.6666666666666666 │
╘══════╧══════╧══════╧══════╧════════════════════╛
               (1,2)  (1,2)   (1,2)  (1,2)

But this is not the definition of dirichlet. The dirichlet definition allows a DIIFERENT set of hyper parameters(pseudo counts) for every column-like this

╒══════╤══════╤══════╤══════╤════════════════════╕
│ A    │ A(0) │ A(0) │ A(1) │ A(1)               │
├──────┼──────┼──────┼──────┼────────────────────┤
│ B    │ B(0) │ B(1) │ B(0) │ B(1)               │
├──────┼──────┼──────┼──────┼────────────────────┤
│ C(0) │ 0.25 │ 0.25 │ 0.5  │ 0.3333333333333333 │
├──────┼──────┼──────┼──────┼────────────────────┤
│ C(1) │ 0.75 │ 0.75 │ 0.5  │ 0.6666666666666666 │
╘══════╧══════╧══════╧══════╧════════════════════╛
               (1,5)   (1,2)  (7,9)   (8,3)

Of course, having the option to put a different set of hyper parameters for different columns(different combination of the target variable's parents) is a necessity because
 in reality you have different priors for different values of a the target variable's parents. For example, in the project I am working on one of the cpds is for a 
variable called "party_identification"(it has 3 states- republican, democrat, and none), and the parents are variables like age, gender, state etc. Obviously, there are different 
priors for P(party_identification-republican | age<30 and gender=female and state=Northeast) 
and for P(party_identification-republican | 50>age>30 and gender=female and state=MIDWEST), and so for those two columns I need 6 different sets of hyper parameters(pseudo counts):
(a_republican_age<30_gender=female,state=northeast), (a_democarat_age<30_gender=female,state=northeast), (a_none_age<30_gender=female,state=northeast)
(a_republican_50>age<30_gender=female,state=midwest), (a_democrat_50>age<30_gender=female,state=midwest), (a_none_50>age<30_gender=female,state=midwest)

but pgmpy-or at least it seems like-only allows 3 sets of hyper parameters(pseudo counts):
(a_republican), (a_democrat), (a_none)

In general, if I have 3 states for the "age" parent, 2 states for the gender variable and 3 states for the state variable, I will need 
3*2*3(parents states) * 3 (target variable state)=54 pseudo counts, or in different words, 3*2*3=18 triplets(a_rep, a_dem, a_none) of pseudo counts, one for each column.
However pgmpy only all allow 3 pseudo counts, or the same triplet (a_rep_a_dem_a_none) for each and every one of the columns.
In general, no matter how big my cpd is, and how many columns (target variable's parents combination) I have pgmpy will only allow the number of hyperparameters to be the number 
of rows (states of the target variable). But it should enable the number of hyperparameters to be number of rows(number of states of the target variables) * number of columns
(parents combination). Or, in other words, different set of pseudo counts (target_variable_state_1......target_variable_state_k) for EACH AND EVERY ONE of the columns.

As I said both from the documentation and the code it seems like pgmpy only allow one set of pseudo counts-a fixed set of pseudo counts-for all the columns.

issue_title: Support glyphOrder custom parameter
issue_body:
It would be helpful if fontmake/glyphsLib honored the glyphOrder custom parameter in Glyphs. This is useful when working with other tools that have a dependency on glyph order.

E.g. Selawik is hinted in VTT, with hint sources saved out to an XML file so I can re-apply hints anytime I rebuild the font. Trouble is, low-level code (e.g. OFFSET[]) has explicit glyph id references, thus once hinted, I need to preserve glyph order. Any new glyphs need to go at the end of the list so as to preserve the glyph IDs of previously hinted glyphs. So, I need some way to control glyph order.

(And, yes, I'd like to update VTT and it's various high/low level languages to support named references, but that's a really big work item).

issue_title: Importing AllenNLP gives limited and confusing logging
Lazy imports in `allennlp/commands/__init__.py`
issue_body:
Would it be possible to not import all of the commands in the `__init__.py` for the commands package?
 An easy fix would be to move the following imports into the `main()` of `__init__.py`:

```python
    from allennlp.commands.elmo import Elmo
    from allennlp.commands.evaluate import Evaluate
    from allennlp.commands.fine_tune import FineTune
    from allennlp.commands.make_vocab import MakeVocab
    from allennlp.commands.predict import Predict
    from allennlp.commands.serve import Serve
    from allennlp.commands.train import Train
    from allennlp.service.predictors import DemoModel
    from allennlp.common.util import import_submodules
```

The apparent error is an import error on `gevent.wsgi` when importing Elmo:

```python
    from allennlp.commands.elmo import ElmoEmbedder
  File "/builds/eng/eloquent/python/lib/ElPy/lib/python3.6/site-packages/allennlp/commands/__init__.py", line 11, in <module>

There's almost certainly another underlying user error here (e.g., I don't have `libevent-dev` on my docker image), but nonetheless it could be nice to be able to import Elmo without having to also import a web server.

issue_title: Allow plugins to provide custom spec parsers
issue_body:
Currently, plugins can optionally provide one Generator class. And one `my_plugin.source-spec.yaml` filetype per generator. This means each `*.source-spec.yaml` filetype requires its own plugin, and generators in separate plugins can't share common processors.

I propose letting plugins provide their own custom spec parsers that extend `parsers.base_parser.BaseParser`. This would allow plugins to resolve source-specs and generators in their own way, potentially allowing plugins to provide more than one generator type, subsequently allowing more than one `*.source-spec.yaml` filetype per plugin.

For example, the `datapackage_pipelines_measure` plugin could have a `social-media` generator, a `website-analytics` generator, a `code-packaging` generator, etc. And each project directory could contain the corresponding `social-media.measure.source-spec.yaml`, `website-analytics.measure.source-spec.yaml`, and `code-packaging.measure.source-spec.yaml` files.

A proposed parser discovery solution:

1. - [ ] [`specs.find_specs()`](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/datapackage-pipelines/blob/4fb7b66bbb0eb63153e0296267a680e788ce782b/datapackage_pipelines/specs/specs.py#L43-L43) looks for more parsers (subclasses of `BaseParser`) in the `parsers` directory of the plugin
2. - [ ] instances of discovered plugin-supplied parsers are prepended to [`specs.SPEC_PARSERS`](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/datapackage-pipelines/blob/4fb7b66bbb0eb63153e0296267a680e788ce782b/datapackage_pipelines/specs/specs.py#L11) (so they take precedence over native parsers).
3. - [ ] `specs.find_specs()` carries on as normal

What do you think, @akariv?

issue_title: X-Forwarded-Proto can be comma-delimited, but router doesn't account for that
issue_body:
This issue supersedes #4504

**TL;DR:** I'm pretty sure we should account for the possibility of multi-valued `X-Forwarded-Proto` headers.

#4504 could not be reproduced for the longest time.  @gerred and I have finally managed to do so, and in the course of doing so, uncovered the root cause.

First a sordid little tale of service discovery:  In Deis v1.x, there is no mechanism for applications (in a micro-service architecture, perhaps) deployed to Deis to discover one another and interact directly.  So in rare cases, some users have deployed apps that talk to one another by routing traffic from one, out to the internet and back into the cluster through the load balancer and the router.  It's not ideal, but it's a workaround real users have used.

Back to our regularly scheduled bug report... Consider the following (actual) scenario: a service proxy deployed to Deis is a facade over numerous other back-end services.  It's implemented using [hapi](http://hapijs.com/).  In this example, hapi is configured to set `X-Forwarded-For` and other, related headers on the outbound HTTPS request.  These headers were already set by the Deis router on the request that was inbound to the service proxy.  hapi copies these headers over from the inbound request to the outbound request, _but also amends them_.  Where the incoming `X-Forwarded-Proto` header may have read `https`, the outbound `X-Forwarded-Proto` header will read `https,https`.

The `X-Forwarded-Proto` header, like it's better known brother `X-Forwarded-For`, is a de-facto header and is not formally described anywhere.  A brief google search has revealed however, that apart from hapi, numerous other frameworks accept or rely on the possibility that the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header might contain a comma-delimited list.  The Deis router is not one of these...

The Deis router, internally, derives a variable named `$access_scheme` from the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header, as depicted here:

```
# trust http_x_forwarded_proto headers correctly indicate ssl offloading
map $http_x_forwarded_proto $access_scheme {
    default $http_x_forwarded_proto;
    ''      $scheme;
}
```

The ultimate intention of this code is to allow the `$access_scheme` variable to assume the value `https`, even if the protocol used for the inbound request was HTTP, _as long as it appears that SSL offloading has already been performed_ (e.g. by the load balancer).

If it is possible for `X-Forwarded-Proto` to bear comma-delimited values, it is also possible for the `$access_scheme` variable to obtain a value such as `https,https`.

When Deis router's `enforceHTTPS` feature is enabled, the `$access_scheme` variable is evaluated in determining if/when a redirect to an insecure URL's secure equivalent should be performed:

```
if ($access_scheme != "https") {
    return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
}
```

In a case such as this, the value `https,https` != `https`, and as a result a redirect is issued... but this will _potentially_ initiate a series of infinite redirects.  In the specific example given, hapi was configured to follow no more than five redirects, so after the sixth, hapi quit and returned a `502` response to the router, which propagated that response back to the client.

Correcting this bug may call for some careful thought.  Some questions:

1. Should SSL offloading be considered to have occurred (the `$access_scheme` variable thereby receiving a value of `https`) if `https` occurs _anywhere_ in the comma-delimited value of the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header?  Or only if the proximate forwarded protocol was `https`?

2. Is the Deis router also guilty of _setting_ this header incorrectly on outbound requests to back-end services?  For instance, if SSL were offloaded by the load balancer, and the request inbound to the router therefore already had the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header with a value of `https`, should the router merely copy that header to the outbound request as it does today?  Or should it amend it as hapi does?  i.e. Should the outbound request have the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header set to `https,https`.  (fwiw, I believe `https,http` would be _incorrect_ since the request was treated by the router _as if_ it were secure.  This seems to be how hapi deals with this as well.)  If we fix this, do we need to / should we _also_ change how we write other `X-Forwarded-*` headers?  `X-Forwarded-For`, for instance, can be comma-delimited as well.

3. Is there an edge case where a request arrives via HTTPS, with an `X-Forwarded-Proto` header of `http`?  Such a case is possible if a client (proxy of some sort) has different opinions than us on how `X-Forwarded-Proto` should be used.  This will trip up our _existing_ logic, because such a request will be treated as insecure, despite having arrived over a secure channel... and that will _also_ trigger the infinite loop described by this issue.

4. Are there technical constraints to correcting this?  Nginx configuration isn't an imperative language, so short of resorting to the Lua module, there are limited options for tackling this.

Whatever is done, the change should also be ported forward to the Deis v2 Router.

issue_title: Calculate Absolute Values and Normalize Fourier Coefficients
issue_body:
I've created a pull request #243 with a rewritten notebook / tutorial / explanatory material for the discrete fourier transformation.
I gained some detailed knowledge and propose 
1) to report the absolute values (Pythagoras on real and imaginary part of the complex number) of the fourier coefficients, which can be calculated via `np.abs`
2) to normalize the DFT coefficients somehow related to the time series length. In detail, I ask to set the parameter `norm="ortho"` in the `np.fft.rfft` function.

I think my motivation gets clear in detail if you scroll through the notebook. One major learning point is the following: **The k-th Fourier Coefficients does correlate the series (with n data points) to a sinusoidal signal with k cycles, sampled with n data points.** For example, if the series is only one period of a sinus, it can be described perfectly by the 1st Fourier coefficient, all others are zero.

In summary, one needs to decide what one is interested in:

- If all series of an investigated dataset are of the same length, normalization is not an issue. If you want to compare time series of different length, raw fourier coefficients cannot be used for comparison of the time series
- Also, if the length of the series is a recorded feature by *tsfresh*, the classifier could (in principle) be capable of learning the "normalized" fourier coefficients, which allow conclusions about the amplitude / absolute value. But this is a nonlinear relationship (it is hyperbolic) which cannot be learned by linear classifiers
- This is a design decision: With the current setup, 
    - it seems that [it is set in the settings](https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/blob/master/tsfresh/feature_extraction/settings.py#L123) to record both the real and imaginary part of the coefficients and to choose the first 100 coefficients
    - in an earlier version of tsfresh, only the first 10 coefficients were taken into account.  This corresponds to establishing some kind of "lowpass" filter.
- White noise (in theory) is present in all frequencies. So, this kind of noise is not restricted to higher frequency bands. However, of one regards higher frequency bands as uninformative (i.e. in some sense as "noise"), it is perfectly fine to reject them and somehow implement a "lowpass" filter by selecting only lower-order coefficients
- If you invoke the FFT calculation in numpy, you get all fourier coefficients. It is up to the feature engineer (tsfresh default-settings engineer) which coefficients shall be reported. Maybe the first 10 (corresponding to the 10 lowest "frequencies", somehow a lowpass) and last 10 coefficients (corresponding to the 10 highest "frequencies", somehow a highpass) are interesting contrary to the first 100 DFT coefficients? I leave this open to discussion.

issue_title: Need a persistent way to keep track of peer details/reputation
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Our `PeerPool` currently provides no API for its clients to choose the _best_  peer when they need to make a request, so they end up picking one randomly.

The `PeerPool` also has to either use a hard-coded list of peers or run the discovery bootstrap from scratch every time it is initialized since we don't have a persistent DB with peer details (i.e. peer ID, chain ID, genesis hash, supported sub-protocols, latest hash/difficulty)

### How can it be fixed

We need to keep peer details/reputation in a persistent store so that the `PeerPool` can reuse known good peers from previous runs as well as provide APIs for clients to pick the _best_ peer for a request.

I'm not sure how to define _best_ here, but I believe a weighted round-robin based on a peer's latency would be a good start. Although we may also need a way to restrict the choices only to those peers that are at least at a certain block height/td

issue_title: Covert .dist-info metadata to conda PrefixRecord
issue_body:
Code around https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/616cf994f71db564047dd420216d2dbcb59010f3/conda/core/prefix_data.py#L250-L254 and https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/616cf994f71db564047dd420216d2dbcb59010f3/conda/core/prefix_data.py#L289-L304 is low-quality PoC code.  It uses imports from `pip._vendor.distlib`.  After working with `distlib`, I don't think the goals it has--and therefore the patterns it uses--are appropriate for our use case here.

The purpose of this task is to convert a `.dist-info` metadata directory to an as-complete-as-possible conda `PrefixRecord`.

issue_title: username and password of proxy being repeatedly asked
issue_body:
First thanks for `conda`, it is a great improvement to distribute python packages over previous solutions.

Using `conda` 3.7.1

I am sitting behind a NTLM proxy at work and as a consequence `conda` keeps asking for my http or https proxy user name and password. See at the end of the post for an example of a session.
In my experience this happens any time conda needs to get through the proxy, being to fetch one  index or download one package. With multiple channels and tens of packages it gets quickly annoying :smile: 

1. Could `conda` somehow cache the username / password after using them successfully?
2. I have also set my `http_proxy` and `https_proxy` to contain both the username and password. They seem to be both discarded, only the proxy url and port are being used by `conda`.

   169                                               elif sys.argv[1] == '..setps1':
   170                                                   # path is a bit of a misnomer here.  It is the prompt setting.  However, it is returned
   171                                                   #    below by printing.  That is why it is named "path"
   172                                                   # DO NOT use os.getenv for this.  One Windows especially, it shows cmd.exe settings
   173                                                   #    for bash shells.  This method uses the shell directly.
   174                                                   path = os.getenv(shelldict['promptvar'], '')
   175                                                   # failsafes
   176                                                   if not path:
   177                                                       if shelldict['exe'] == 'cmd.exe':
   178                                                           path = '$P$G'
   179                                                   # strip off previous prefix, if any:
   180                                                   path = re.sub(".*\(\(.*\)\)\ ", "", path, count=1)
   181                                                   env_path = sys.argv[3]
   182                                                   if changeps1 and env_path:
   183                                                       path = "(({0})) {1}".format(os.path.split(env_path)[-1], path)

issue_title: Invalid root_path for pip installed conda on ubuntu 14.04
issue_body:
These lines https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4b51cd79bf17f59d1c1db95eeae539b4831baba9/conda/config.py#L115-L116 expect sys.prefix be the same as conda's prefix.  This is not true on ubuntu 14.04.

```
$ which python
/usr/bin/python
$ which conda
/usr/local/bin/conda
```

As a result, the symlink in an environment points to /usr/bin/conda, which does not exist.  The if-condition in https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4b51cd79bf17f59d1c1db95eeae539b4831baba9/conda/install.py#L387-L388 is True and the os.symlink call will raise an exception due to "OSError: File exists".

It seems like this can be workaround by setting CONDA_ROOT=/usr/local

issue_title: Add an option to remove spaces around the assignment delimiter (i.e. allow '=' instead of ' = ')
issue_body:
Many INI files are created w/o spaces around the delimiter between the 'variables' and 'assigned values'.

Looking at the `_write_line` function, it seems that a variable, i.e. `assignment_delim`, could be used instead of the hardcoded `' = '`.  When a ConfigObj is initialized, it can set `assignment_delim` to `' = '` by default, but allow for it to be overridden.

This would allow for writing to many more types of INI/CONF files.  Hopefully this can be implemented.

issue_title: Allow user to specify the version of json command output
issue_body:
Some commands have multiple output versions.  The first (version 1) is the default unless otherwise specified in the request. Example: `show management cvx`.

Valid values: `r'^\d+|latest$'`

kwargs are passed all the way up to the request method which can override the default (1) to a specified value:
https://github.com/arista-eosplus/pyeapi/blob/5470f3b16fac830b101db35ca32068e62abb0162/pyeapi/eapilib.py#L299

issue_title: Update Documentation to include autoComplete Example
[API BGP] _parse_shutdown can't accurately determine shutdown state
issue_body: The parse was using a simple check for 'no neighbor NAME shutdown'.  This isn't satisfactory.  The neighbor can be active, or idle and the configuration omits 'no neighbor NAME shutdown'  This was causing pyeapi to report the neighbor was shutdown when it really isn't.  Some more research needs to be done here since there's also a shutdown state for the peer_group (if you add the neighbor to it).

issue_title: [Monitor] Table/TSV Output useless for Metric/Metric-definition list commands
issue_body:
`az monitor metric-definitions list` is a "cataloging" endpoint for displaying information that can be used to create alerts.  The table output for this command (on a VM) looks like:

```
PrimaryAggregationType    ResourceGroup    ResourceId                                                                                                                 Unit
------------------------  ---------------  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  --------------
Average                   tjp-vm           /subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590/resourceGroups/tjp-vm/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm1  Percent
Total                     tjp-vm           /subscriptions/0b1f6471-1bf0-4dda-aec3-cb9272f09590/resourceGroups/tjp-vm/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm1  Bytes
```

Completely useless.

`az monitor metrics list` actually displays metric values over a period of time.  The table format for this looks like:

```
ResourceGroup    Unit
---------------  -------
tjp-vm           Percent
tjp-vm           Bytes
```

Also useless.

I recommend an output transformer (so that it will apply to table and TSV) to put these into some kind of useful format.

issue_title: [Network] Update Commands not at parity with Xplat
issue_body:
The vast majority of the networking `update` commands rely on the generic update (`add, set, remove`) to achieve their functionality. However, this means that when compared to Xplat, they are missing several convenience options and thus, while at technical parity, might not be considered at "UX parity" (if that's a thing) by users and/or service teams. The following commands have this issue:

- [ ] public-ip
- [ ] traffic-manager profile
- [ ] traffic-manager endpoint
- [ ] route-table route
- [ ] local-gateway
- [ ] vpn-gateway
- [ ] vpn-connection
- [ ] application-gateway
- [ ] express-route circuit
- [ ] express-route peering

```

issue_title: Move docs (at least partially) to netlify
issue_body:
The docs site should move to netlify under aiohttp's own domain.

Even if you're not keen to move the deployed docs, at least you should set up netlify to build pull requests so the docs can easily be checked for each PR.

Advantages of moving from readthedocs to netlify:
* aiohttp can have (would need) it's own domain, perhaps shared for other aiolibs projects
* deploying docs for new releases can be automated through CI
* No ads on the aiohttp docs, or if the project thinks it's worthwhile aiohttp docs could host our own
* netlify's feature for building all pull requests makes checking docs look right very easy for collaborators and reviewers

Disadvantages:
* someone needs to buy a domain for aiohttp
* a redirect would be required from the current site. How easy does readthedocs make this?

@asvetlov If you think this would be a good idea, I think I can setup a netlify site for my fork and see if it works? If it does you can setup the main site.

issue_title: Client: _request.timeout cancels the current AND the next request
issue_body:
## Long story short `_request.timeout` will cancel the current request AND the next request. ## Expected behaviour I expected it to behave the same way as `aiohttp.Timeout`. In other words it should not leak the cancellation to the next request. ## Actual behaviour - `_request.timeout` will call `TimeoutHandle.__call__` which runs `TimeoutHandle._callbacks` which runs `TimerContext.timeout` which sets `self._cancelled=True` and somehow it leads to the cancellation of the next call of the coroutine too. - `aiohttp.Timeout` does NOT call `TimeoutHandle.__call__` ## Steps to reproduce Run a local server on port 8888 (I left a sample code for it below). Make it slow to cause timeout errors from the client. In this example 2 seconds cause timeout error. In order to see the difference between the behaviors, switch from `fetch = fetch_cancells` to `fetch = fetch_works` and vice verca. ### Client ```py import asyncio import aiohttp async def fetch_cancells(session, url): async with session.get(url, timeout=2) as response: return response.status, await response.text() async def fetch_works(session, url): with aiohttp.Timeout(2, loop=session.loop): async with session.get(url) as response: return response.status, await response.text() # Change this line to fetch=fetch_works to see it working. fetch = fetch_cancells # fetch = fetch_works print(fetch.__name__) async def hit_url(loop): print('Hitting') async with aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=loop) as session: status, body = await fetch(session, 'http://localhost:8888') return status, body async def master(loop): print('----------') while True: try: status, body = await hit_url(loop) except asyncio.CancelledError: print('Cancelled!') except asyncio.TimeoutError: print('Time Out!!') except Exception: raise else: print(status) print(body) # await asyncio.sleep(.5) # <-- def main(): loop = asyncio.get_event_loop() loop.set_debug(True) try: loop.run_until_complete(master(loop)) finally: loop.close() if __name__ == '__main__': main() ``` ### Results This is what you get with `fetch = fetch_works`, aka expected behavior: ``` fetch_works ---------- Hitting TimerContext._cancelled=False Time Out!! ---------- Hitting TimerContext._cancelled=False Time Out!! ---------- Hitting TimerContext._cancelled=False Time Out!! ``` And this is when `fetch=fetch_cancells` ``` fetch_cancells ---------- Hitting TimeoutHandle.__call__ is called TimerContext.timeout is called TimerContext._cancelled=True TimerContext._cancelled=True Time Out!! ---------- Hitting TimerContext._cancelled=False Cancelled! ---------- Hitting TimeoutHandle.__call__ is called TimerContext.timeout is called TimerContext._cancelled=True TimerContext._cancelled=True Time Out!! hi!

POST!

issue_title: request.json() incorrectly assumes some json responses are text. (from github raw links to json files)
issue_body:
## Long story short

<!-- Please describe your problem and why the fix is important. -->
Some json files (those on github and requested by their raw links) are assumed to be ``text`` when they are really json.

## Expected behaviour

<!-- What is the behaviour you expect? -->
aoihttp to correctly identify the data in the raw github url is in fact json.

## Actual behaviour

<!-- What's actually happening? -->
Some json files (those on github and requested by their raw links) are assumed to be ``text`` when they are really json.

## Steps to reproduce

<!-- Please describe steps to reproduce the issue.
     If you have a script that does that please include it here within
     markdown code markup -->
1. create an github repo with json files.
2. get raw links to them.
3. use ``ClientSession.get`` on them.
4. try to use ``r.json()`` on the response.

## Your environment

<!-- Describe the environment you have that lead to your issue.
     This includes project version, OS, proxy server and other bits that
     are related to your case. -->
Python 3.6.0rc1
Windows 7 Ultimate SP1 build 7601

## Example that can be reporduced

version that fails:

```py
import aiohttp
import asyncio


class HTTPClient:
    """test HTTP client."""
    def __init__(self, loop=None):
        self.loop = loop if loop is not None else asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=self.loop)

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def request(self, *args, **kwargs):
        yield from self.session.request(*args, **kwargs)

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def get(self, *args, **kwargs):
        data = yield from self.session.get(*args, **kwargs)
        return data

    def close(self):
        self.session.close()


async def get_json(loop=None):
    session = HTTPClient(loop=loop)
    data = await session.get("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/AraHaan/DecoraterBot-Plugins/master/pluginlist.json")
    r = await data.json()  # aiohttp fails here.
    print(r)
    session.close()

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(get_json(loop=loop))
```

version that does not complain but is of incorrect type for me (not an json object that I would need to get):

```py
import aiohttp
import asyncio


class HTTPClient:
    """test HTTP client."""
    def __init__(self, loop=None):
        self.loop = loop if loop is not None else asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=self.loop)

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def request(self, *args, **kwargs):
        yield from self.session.request(*args, **kwargs)

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def get(self, *args, **kwargs):
        data = yield from self.session.get(*args, **kwargs)
        return data

    def close(self):
        self.session.close()


async def get_json(loop=None):
    session = HTTPClient(loop=loop)
    data = await session.get("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/AraHaan/DecoraterBot-Plugins/master/pluginlist.json")
    r = await data.text()
    print(r)
    session.close()

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(get_json(loop=loop))
```

Maybe what there needs to be is something to check if the response data looks to be json and if so make it so I can use ``r.json()`` on the response.

issue_title: Insecure algorithm for determining remote/scheme/host
[Client] Boolean value in headers raises error
issue_body:
## Long story short

When passing a boolean as a value in headers an exception is raised.

For example this command:

```py
with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        res = yield from session.get('https://google.com', headers={'MyHeader': True})
```

raises the following exception:

```py
File "/.virtualenvs/aiohttp_headers/lib/python3.5/site-packages/aiohttp/http_writer.py", line 250, in <listcomp>
    [k + SEP + v + END for k, v in headers.items()])
TypeError: Can't convert 'bool' object to str implicitly
```

## Expected behaviour

The 'pythonic' expectation would be to pass dictionaries with boolean values instead of having to pass as string.

## Actual behaviour

Currently the dict passed to headers has to be:

```py
{'MyHeader': 'true'}
```

## Steps to reproduce

Run the following script:

```py
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import asyncio
import aiohttp


@asyncio.coroutine
def main():
    with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        res = yield from session.get('https://google.com', headers={'MyHeader': True})


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main())

```

## Your environment

Ubuntu 16.04/17.04 
aiohttp 2.2.5

issue_title: Improving Accuracy and Rejecting Noise with mod_pocketsphinx on Freeswitch
issue_body:
We are using mod_pocketsphinx integration with Freeswitch to perform a simple "accept" call feature. Here is the grammar:
-----


#JSGF V1.0;
/**
  * JSGF Grammar for PTA
  */
grammar call_accept;

public <command> = <keyword>;

<keyword> = (accept);

-------
While testing a set of sample captures (.pcap) we found a very high false positive rate for the following cases:

gibberish.pcap with random words (https://www.dropbox.com/s/68b2ju7ha3i34mg/gibberish.pcap?dl=0)

background_music.pcap with a music played in the background (https://www.dropbox.com/s/h1yzd4arkcwybaw/background_music.pcap?dl=0)

**Note:** Decode the above pcap file as "RTP" on wireshark.

Also, we found that other words such as "okay" and "yes" are wrongly detected as "accept". We see that confidence is 100% in all those cases.

How do we improve this scenario? Should we define an extensive grammar?

We followed: https://sourceforge.net/p/cmusphinx/discussion/help/thread/1c6cb941/?limit=50#efa9 with no luck in mod_pocketsphinx for Freeswitch. 

Any thoughts/suggestion?

issue_title: Selecting downwards to markdown cell is broken
issue_body:
1. Create a new notebook.
2. Make a bunch of cells, and convert them to markdown mode.
3. Select a cell other than the last cell, and ensure you are in command mode.
4. Press shift-down to extend the selection downwards.

Expected result: the cell selected in step 3 and the cell immediately below that are highlighted.

Actual result: the cursor moves down (as if I wasn't holding shift).

* Replicated on master with Firefox and Chromium
* It seems to be the visual highlighting going wrong; changing the cell type still affects all the cells that should be selected.
* The deciding thing seems to be if the cell the selection is extending *to* is a markdown cell: I can select down from a markdown cell to a code cell, but not down from a code cell to a markdown cell.
* There is another bug when a range has been selected going up from the bottom, and then you press up or down without shift. I hope they're related and will both be fixed at once, but if not, I'll report that in more detail.

issue_title: Pytest failing on error that is handled in app/__init__.py
issue_body:
I have a custom error I raise within one resource in my flask app. It's defined as such:
```
class BotAlreadyExists(Exception):
    status_code = 400

    def __init__(self, message='Bot already exists for this slack team', status_code=None, payload=None):
        Exception.__init__(self)
        self.message = message
        if status_code is not None:
            self.status_code = status_code
        self.payload = payload

    def to_dict(self):
        rv = dict(self.payload or ())
        rv['message'] = self.message
        return rv
```
In `app/__init__.py`, I use a decorator to handle this exception so that I get a 400 instead of 500 error as well as a useful message.
```
    @app.errorhandler(BotAlreadyExists)
    def handle_bot_already_exists_usage(error):
        response = jsonify(error.to_dict())
        response.status_code = error.status_code
        return response
```
This works perfectly when running locally, however, when I run a functional test using pytest / pytest-flask, it's as if the decorator never has an effect. Rather than returning the error message with a HTTP 400 status, the test fails because of the exception. Why is the flask app correctly handling the exception locally but failing to when testing?

issue_title: xdist chooses the wrong package version
issue_body:
When installing two different versions of a package in the same environment, `xdist` doesn't pick the same version as regular `pytest`.

---
**Steps to reproduce using *Invoke* package for instance:**

    virtualenv ENV && source ENV/bin/activate
    pip install pytest pytest-xdist invoke==0.11.0

Download the [source](https://github.com/pyinvoke/invoke/releases/tag/0.12.2) of Invoke 0.12.2 and install it with `python setup.py install`.

I have a dummy test file `test_version.py`, containing:

~~~python
import invoke

def test_version():
    assert False, str(invoke.__version__)
~~~

When running `py.test test_version.py`, we see that the test is running with Invoke 0.12.2:

~~~
============================ test session starts =============================
platform darwin -- Python 2.7.10, pytest-2.9.2, py-1.4.31, pluggy-0.3.1
rootdir: /Users/jcruy/Desktop/xdist_bug, inifile: 
plugins: xdist-1.14
collected 1 items 

test_version.py F

================================== FAILURES ==================================
________________________________ test_version ________________________________

    def test_version():
>       assert False, str(invoke.__version__)
E       AssertionError: 0.12.2
E       assert False

test_version.py:5: AssertionError
========================== 1 failed in 0.09 seconds ==========================
~~~

However, when running it with `xdist` (`py.test test_version.py -n 2`), we see that the test is running with Invoke 0.11.0:

~~~
============================ test session starts =============================
platform darwin -- Python 2.7.10, pytest-2.9.2, py-1.4.31, pluggy-0.3.1
rootdir: /Users/jcruy/Desktop/xdist_bug, inifile: 
plugins: xdist-1.14
gw0 [1] / gw1 [1]
scheduling tests via LoadScheduling
F
================================== FAILURES ==================================
________________________________ test_version ________________________________
[gw1] darwin -- Python 2.7.10 /Users/jcruy/Desktop/xdist_bug/ENV/bin/python
def test_version():
>       assert False, str(invoke.__version__)
E       AssertionError: 0.11.0
E       assert False

test_version.py:5: AssertionError
========================== 1 failed in 0.45 seconds ==========================
~~~

---

**Notes:**
- I chose the Invoke package for example's sake only, it happens with other packages as well.
- I know that it's not a really good thing to install two versions of a packages in the same environment, but I believe that the behaviour between `pytest` and `xdist` should at least be consistent.
- I would gladly contribute to a fix, if you point me to the area of the code which might be responsible for that.

**Environment:**
- OS X 10.11.4
- Python 2.7.10
- pytest 2.9.2
- pytest-xdist 1.14

issue_title: Referencing netstandard1.6 package in .NET 4.6.1
issue_body:
This issue is about a reference resolution/loading error.  It seemed to me that it's not `cli` related because I'm not using the .NET Core tools, not `corefx` related because it doesn't relate to a particular API, and not `coreclr` because that seems too low-level.. but I'm not sure so please educate me if I'm in the wrong place - thanks :)

`dotnet` version  1.2.0-beta-001297-00
VS 2017 version 15.3.0 preview 3.0 (i.e. has .NET Standard 2.0 tooling)

I have (maintain, build, publish) a NuGet package which targets only `netstandard1.6`.

I have a .NET 4.6.1 web application which references it.

I get the IIS error:

`Could not load file or assembly 'System.Runtime, Version=4.1.0.0, Culture=neutral, PublicKeyToken=b03f5f7f11d50a3a' or one of its dependencies. The located assembly's manifest definition does not match the assembly reference. (Exception from HRESULT: 0x80131040)`

The NuGet package's deps.json file lists only `System.Runtime` `4.3`, no references to `4.1`.  However, the assembly itself does reference `4.1.0.0` (checking via ILSpy).  I don't know why - the project file is straightforward, targets just netstandard1.6, some `1.1.1` versions of `Microsoft.AspNetCore.*` and `Microsoft.Extensions.Options` packages and doesn't have any other magic.  It does however reference 3 other NuGet packages in my little system.

One of those NuGet package projects also targets `netstandard1.6` but also `net451` (and conditionally uses the `Microsoft.TargetingPack.NETFramework.v4.5.1` for the net451 build).  The net451 build also conditionally has an (unversioned) reference to `System.Runtime` (not a package or project reference).  I have no idea if this is relevant, however.

I'd just like to know what the recommended approach or next steps for tracking down the problem here would be - or how can I educate myself about this further?  I get the concept of .NET Standard as an "interface", but don't grasp the entire bigger ecosystem picture in terms of these additional references. Other (.NET Core) consumers of the main NuGet package work just fine.  

(note: as per https://github.com/dotnet/standard/issues/295, I do have a reference to NETStandard.Library 1.6.1 on my net461 consumer project)

issue_title: Invalid Content Disposition Header
issue_body:
According to https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Disposition the parameters of the content disposition header must be quoted. Instead APS.Net Core sends them without quote:

`attachment; filename=latest_bin.txt; filename*=UTF-8''latest_bin.txt`

I'm using .Net Core 2.0

The part of the code which fetches the result from the task object fails to work when we run the .net core code from VS 2017 with the following exception:

   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
   at System.Threading.Tasks.RendezvousAwaitable`1.GetResult()
   at System.Net.Http.WinHttpHandler.<StartRequest>d__105.MoveNext()
   --- End of inner exception stack trace ---
   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
   at System.Runtime.CompilerServices.TaskAwaiter.HandleNonSuccessAndDebuggerNotification(Task task)
   at System.ServiceModel.Channels.ServiceModelHttpMessageHandler.<SendAsync>d__41.MoveNext()
   --- End of stack trace from previous location where exception was thrown ---
   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
   at System.Runtime.CompilerServices.TaskAwaiter.HandleNonSuccessAndDebuggerNotification(Task task)
   at System.Runtime.CompilerServices.ConfiguredTaskAwaitable`1.ConfiguredTaskAwaiter.GetResult()
   at System.Net.Http.HttpClient.<FinishSendAsyncUnbuffered>d__59.MoveNext()
   --- End of stack trace from previous location where exception was thrown ---
   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
   at System.Runtime.CompilerServices.TaskAwaiter.HandleNonSuccessAndDebuggerNotification(Task task)
   at System.ServiceModel.Channels.HttpChannelFactory`1.HttpClientRequestChannel.HttpClientChannelAsyncRequest.<SendRequestAsync>d__13.MoveNext()
   --- End of inner exception stack trace ---
   at System.Runtime.AsyncResult.End[TAsyncResult](IAsyncResult result)
   at System.ServiceModel.Channels.ServiceChannel.SendAsyncResult.End(SendAsyncResult result)
   at System.ServiceModel.Channels.ServiceChannel.EndCall(String action, Object[] outs, IAsyncResult result)
   at System.ServiceModel.Channels.ServiceChannelProxy.TaskCreator.<>c__DisplayClass1_0.<CreateGenericTask>b__0(IAsyncResult asyncResult)}
		
		{System.Net.Http.HttpRequestException: An error occurred while sending the request. ---> System.Net.Http.WinHttpException: The operation timed out
		   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
		   at System.Threading.Tasks.RendezvousAwaitable`1.GetResult()
		   at System.Net.Http.WinHttpHandler.<StartRequest>d__105.MoveNext()
		   --- End of inner exception stack trace ---
		   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
		   at System.Runtime.CompilerServices.TaskAwaiter.HandleNonSuccessAndDebuggerNotification(Task task)
		   at System.ServiceModel.Channels.ServiceModelHttpMessageHandler.<SendAsync>d__41.MoveNext()
		   --- End of stack trace from previous location where exception was thrown ---
		   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
		   at System.Runtime.CompilerServices.TaskAwaiter.HandleNonSuccessAndDebuggerNotification(Task task)
		   at System.Runtime.CompilerServices.ConfiguredTaskAwaitable`1.ConfiguredTaskAwaiter.GetResult()
		   at System.Net.Http.HttpClient.<FinishSendAsyncUnbuffered>d__59.MoveNext()
		   --- End of stack trace from previous location where exception was thrown ---
		   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
		   at System.Runtime.CompilerServices.TaskAwaiter.HandleNonSuccessAndDebuggerNotification(Task task)
		   at System.ServiceModel.Channels.HttpChannelFactory`1.HttpClientRequestChannel.HttpClientChannelAsyncRequest.<SendRequestAsync>d__13.MoveNext()}
				
				{System.Net.Http.WinHttpException (0x80072EE2): The operation timed out
				   at System.Runtime.ExceptionServices.ExceptionDispatchInfo.Throw()
				   at System.Threading.Tasks.RendezvousAwaitable`1.GetResult()
				   at System.Net.Http.WinHttpHandler.<StartRequest>d__105.MoveNext()}

We are running the code on a corporation pc which I guess is behind the proxy but the thing is that the legacy code from the same computer but run within VS 2015 works just as expected. 
It became even more confusing when we tested the same code (.net core 2.0) in VS Code on a cloud (AWS) based computer and it worked! 
Putting these all together I guess(ed) the new VS or .net core 2.0 runtime is somehow blocked by some firewall policy and tried different options like enabling a proxy for the app we are working with or for the whole VS 2017 (devenv process) and disabling some firewall domains (those that we could) but neither one worked. 
My assumption is that .net Core runtime is somehow blocked from connecting to the internet.
Yet another observation: 
I tested the following code on a local machine (behind the company firwall) on .net core 2.0 and from VS 2017 and it also worked fine:

`        public static async Task<byte[]> DownloadFile(string url)
        {
            using (var client = new HttpClient())
            {
                using (var result = await client.GetAsync(url))
                {
                    if (result.IsSuccessStatusCode)
                    {
                        return await result.Content.ReadAsByteArrayAsync();
                    }

                }
            }
            return null;
        }
`
So looks like it is only the outgoing requests that are blocked.

issue_title: Can't import _io (requires deleting old virtualenv)
issue_body:
Someone on the community forums came across a problem with Python refusing to import `_io` a while ago:

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/cant-use-letsencrypt-auto-importerror-no-module-named--io/2345/3

The solution required deleting and recreating the virtualenv. Unfortunately, people are still running into this; someone reported #2329 today which was exactly the same problem (with the same solution). Why does this happen, and how can we make it so that people don't have to recreate their virtualenvs?

issue_title: Use pytest fixtures rather than instantiating new objects
issue_body:
```
snafu$ for w in 'connect(' VariantMapper AssemblyMapper Normalizer Validator; do printf "%d $w\n" $(fgrep $w tests/*.py|wc -l); done

18 connect(
16 VariantMapper
15 AssemblyMapper
11 Normalizer
10 Validator
```

These are the *minimum* number of times we connect or instantiate these objects for tests. (The above excludes cases where we instantiate in loops, if any.)

Instead, use common fixtures that are created once per test session.

issue_title: Allow articles to have a featured video
issue_body:
🚨  🚨  **PREREQUISITE: #746** 🚨  🚨  

We want to extend the Article model to support a `featured_video` field in addition to the existing `featured_image` field.

Backend requirements:
- Add a `featured_video` field to the [Article model](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/modules/content/models.py#L246)
  - Should be a foreign key to a new `VideoAttachment` model, similar to [ImageAttachment](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/modules/content/models.py#L480-L490)
- Extend the [ArticleSerializer](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/api/serializers.py#L388) to support the new `featured_video` field

Frontend requirements:
- Add an interface to the article editor that allows the user to set a featured video (in addition to a featured image)
  - This can be implemented as a new input called `VideoSelectInput`
  - Unlike the [ImageInput](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/inputs/ImageInput.js), it doesn't need to open the image manager modal and can just be a simple `ItemSelectInput` (similar to [ArticleSelectInput](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/inputs/selects/ArticleSelectInput.js))
  - After `VideoSelectInput` is created, it can be added to the [FeaturedImageTab](https://github.com/ubyssey/dispatch/blob/develop/dispatch/static/manager/src/js/components/Editor/tabs/FeaturedImageTab.js) component of the article editor

issue_title: Resolve an array of schema objects in parameters
issue_body:
I see that the marshmallow plugin currently resolves an array of schema items in the response, but is there existing functionality to also do this in the request?

I'd like to be able to define my request parameters a docstring like this:

```
spec.definition('Schema', schema=Schema)

def example():
    """Example view that accepts an array objects 
    ---
    post:
        parameters:
            - in: body
              schema:
                type: array
                items: Schema
    """
```

So that `['parameters'][0]['schema']` will produce more explicit results like `{'type': 'array', 'items': {'$ref': '#/definitions/Schema'}}` rather than `{'type': 'array', 'items': 'Schema'}`.

Does this functionality already exist? If not, I am happy to submit a PR to address this. Thank you!

issue_title: Empty Only Treated as None
issue_body:
When the `only` parameter is an empty list/set, it causes all the fields to be de/serialized like `None`. The implementation of 2.x and 3.0 are not in compliance with their respective docs.

> **only** (_tuple_) – A list or tuple of fields to serialize. If **None**, all fields will be serialized.

```py
class TestSchema(Schema):
    foo = fields.Field()
sch = TestSchema(only=())
data = dict(foo='bar')
result = sch.dump(data)
assert 'foo' not in result

# assert 'foo' not in {'foo': 'bar'}
```

This could create a security vulnerability if an application was dynamically generating the field set based on security role. A filter that was meant to hide everything would inadvertently show everything.

issue_title: Removing rows in bigger than memory datasets
issue_body:
I have a .loom file of 33694 rows (genes) x 632571 columns (cells), so keep in mind that this volume of data cannot fit into the available memory. I want to do some gene filtering and thus removing some of them. I know that for performance reasons, deleting rows directly is not allowed, so I tried to use the `loompy.scan()` for `axis=0`. Here's a minimal example:

```
import loompy
import numpy as np

ds = loompy.connect('my_data.loom', 'r')

cg_exp = ds.map([np.count_nonzero], axis=0)

genes = np.where(cg_exp > 2)[0]
for (ix, selection, view) in ds.scan(items=genes, axis=0):
    loompy.create_append('gene_filtered_ds.loom', view.layers, view.ra, view.ca)
```

This throws:

```
/home/user/anaconda3/envs/tensorflow/lib/python3.6/site-packages/h5py/__init__.py:36: FutureWarning: Conversion of the second argument of issubdtype from `float` to `np.floating` is deprecated. In future, it will be treated as `np.float64 == np.dtype(float).type`.
  from ._conv import register_converters as _register_converters
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "gene_filtering.py", line 10, in <module>
    loompy.create_append('gene_filtered_ds.loom', view.layers, view.ra, view.ca)
  File "/home/user/anaconda3/envs/tensorflow/lib/python3.6/site-packages/loompy/loompy.py", line 807, in create_append
    ds.add_columns(layers, col_attrs, fill_values=fill_values)
  File "/home/user/anaconda3/envs/tensorflow/lib/python3.6/site-packages/loompy/loompy.py", line 281, in add_columns
    raise ValueError(f"Layer {layer} has {matrix.shape[0]} rows but file has {self.shape[0]}")
ValueError: Layer  has 460 rows but file has 415
```

I can see two issues in my code:

1. the `loompy.create_append()` tries to append columns to the file, which is of course the wrong way to go,
2. it tries to match the rows, but the genes in each chunk are different (both as genes per se and as number of genes passed the QC).

I played around with the `loompy.scan()` since from the documentation it seems able to return batches of either rows or columns, however I couldn't find any good way to write the data on a file iteratively. Is there any solution to this?

issue_title: BCECriterion may return NaN
issue_body:
BCECriterion may return NaN, caused by #2231 .
#2231 speed up BCECriterion, but import a new bug may return Nan.
The changing in line 84,
```
old : buffer.fill(ev.fromType(1.0)).sub(input).add(ev.fromType(eps)).log()
new:  buffer.fill(ev.fromType(1.0 + eps)).sub(input).log()
```
For float version BCE, `ev.fromType(1.0 + eps)` (while `eps = 1e-12`) will convert the Double to Float, but `(1.0 + 1e-12).toFloat`'s result is `1.0`, eps won't work for float version BCE.

issue_title: critical error in singlediode p_mp calculation
issue_body:
Bad news. I discovered a major error in the singlediode p_mp calculation: given a time series input, the numerical solver exits if the solution for any single time is within the error tolerance, rather than requiring that the solution for all times be close enough. The error is in [this line](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/5218c9d3dd2d5a4e410140de3180b7ef00e8b1ee/pvlib/pvsystem.py#L1728). 

Here's some sample code:

```python
photocurrent = np.linspace(0, 10, 11)
resistance_shunt = 16
resistance_series = 0.094
nNsVth = 0.473
saturation_current = 1.943e-09

sd = pvsystem.singlediode(photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth)

sd['i_mp']
array([ 0.        ,  0.59453544,  1.56145498,  2.54105091,  3.52353436,
        4.50658749,  5.4890739 ,  6.47016579,  7.44909413,  8.42504244,
        9.39708533])

# change .all() to .any()

pvsystem = reload(pvlib.pvsystem)

sd_any = pvsystem.singlediode(photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series, resistance_shunt, nNsVth)

sd_any['i_mp']
array([ 0.        ,  0.54538398,  1.43273966,  2.36328163,  3.29255606,
        4.23101358,  5.16177031,  6.09368251,  7.02197553,  7.96846051,
        8.88220557])

sd['i_mp']/sd_any['i_mp']
array([        nan,  1.09012266,  1.0898386 ,  1.07522137,  1.07015167,
        1.06513189,  1.06340917,  1.06178256,  1.06082599,  1.05729864,
        1.05796756])

sd['p_mp']/sd_any['p_mp']
array([        nan,  0.98250248,  0.92482996,  0.91242588,  0.91052337,
        0.91256532,  0.9165425 ,  0.92159883,  0.92731275,  0.93339723,
        0.93974898])
```

So the error here is on the order of 5 to 10%, but the results will depend on your exact input.

The blue dots on the IV curve below show the p_mp points in the old code. The black dots show the p_mp points in fixed code. These IV curves are generated using a fix for #83 that I'll make a PR for soon. They're also what led me to discovering the problem.

![ivcurves](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/4383303/17006598/cedaa96c-4e96-11e6-9e9a-64ed1377536c.png) 

On a related note, someone should put more effort into verifying that the matlab and python libraries give the same results. Not me.

issue_title: API request: force close on context
issue_body:
Hi,

As far as I understand, `Z3_del_context` does not really delete the context if some formulas still reference it.
Is it possible to have `Z3_force_del_context` or similar which would _really_ delete the context and all the associated data no matter what?

It would be really handy from Java, if the client does not want to keep track of the used formulas, yet wants to enforce the fact that once all contexts are closed, no more memory is occupied by Z3.

issue_title: Java api memory leak?
issue_body: I was looking at the JNI code for the java api (in Native.cpp) and noticed a number of calls to `GetStringUTFChars` without corresponding calls to `ReleaseStringUTFChars`. At best that will result in a memory leak. I haven't tracked down the use of the resulting allocated array, but there is no guarantee that the JVM returns a piece of memory obtained from `malloc`, thus you cannot safely free the memory. Furthermore, if the JVM is running with native memory tracking turned on, then the memory allocated by `GetStringUTFChars` will not be safe to pass to `free`.

issue_title: NULL (SQL) values for VARCHAR cols are not properly converted in pd.DataFrame
issue_body:
The above title is a big misleading since at the core of the issue is the translation of the dshape to the numpy dtype. However the title reflects (IMHO) a common use case for odo: (sql) Table <-> (pandas) DataFrame.
When odo "discovers" a nullable varchar column of a given length (n) it will create a datashape (datashape type): ?string[n]. For example a VARCHAR(10) NULL is ?string[10].
When odo "converts" the (sql) Table to the (pandas) DataFrame, it will first convert the Table to an Iterator, then chunksize it, move the chunk to numpy array, to a chunked DataFrame to then build the final DataFrame. The trip to numpy is the issue.

To create the numpy array with the correct dtype, odo translates the datashape into a valid numpy dtype. Here is our example:
`>>>odo.numpy_dtype.unit_to_dtype('?string[10]')`
`dtype('<U10')`

If the values are not string but None instead the dtype 'U10' will translate the None as the string 'None'.
Here's a example:
```
np.array(['a', None, 'b'], dtype='<U10')
Out[1]: 
array(['a', 'None', 'b'], 
      dtype='<U10')
```
Here's another example with a conversion of a collection to pandas with a datashape corresponding to nullable varchar:
```
import pandas as pd
ds = odo.dshape("3 * {c1: ?string[1], c2:?string[1], c3: ?string[1]}")
df = odo.odo([('a', None, 'b'), ('c', None, 'd'), ('e', 'f', 'g')], pd.DataFrame, dshape=ds)
df.ix[0].c2
Out[2]: 'N'
```
Note that if the datashape was simply ?string then odo would translate as dtype('O') and the None value would therefore be kept.

I'm not sure how to fix it properly. My use case is the conversion from sql to pandas and therefore the odo.backends.discover_sqlalchemy_column function could be "fixed" by testing the nullability of the column and creating a datashape without the type.length.
It may not be the optimum way.

Note also that given that pandas provides its own .read_sql_table and .to_sql those "native" pandas functions/methods could be registered in odo convert (and avoid the numpy trip).

issue_title: Expand GET/POST search tests
issue_body:
Pulp supports searching via both GET and POST requests. When searching with a GET request, parameters should be passed in via the URL, and when searching with a POST request, parameters should be passed in the body of the request. (This is appropriate behaviour, by the way. Network intermediaries are at liberty to discard the body of any GET request.) Unfortunately, this makes Pulp's search facilities more complicated and, therefore, more prone to bugs.

Tests should be written that execute identical GET and POST searches and verify that the results are identical. Much of the searching code is very generic and applies no matter what kind of entity is being searched for. As a result, even adding a few tests should help immensely with preventing regressions.

Further documentation on the GET and POST search queries is available [here](https://pulp.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dev-guide/conventions/criteria.html#search-api).

issue_title: AsyncioExecutor produces hard to debug errors
issue_body:
When an `async` resolver is raising an exception, this exception is not logged and its stack in lost and never printed. The only "report" is the error carried by the `ExecutionResult` but we can only access its type and message, not the actual stack trace.

This is different for sync resolvers (even when using the `AsyncioExecutor`): the exception is printed by `graphql.execution.executor resolve_or_error`.

Below is an example to illustrate the issue:
```python
from graphql.execution.executors.asyncio import AsyncioExecutor
from graphql import GraphQLObjectType, GraphQLField, GraphQLString, GraphQLSchema, graphql

def resolve_hello(root, args, context, info):
    raise Exception("wololo")

async def resolve_hello_async(root, args, context, info):
    raise Exception("wololo")

QueryType = GraphQLObjectType("Query", fields={
    "hello": GraphQLField(GraphQLString, resolver=resolve_hello),
    "helloAsync": GraphQLField(GraphQLString, resolver=resolve_hello_async),
})

schema = GraphQLSchema(query=QueryType)

print("Sync execution")

result = graphql(schema, '{ hello }', executor=AsyncioExecutor())
# This will log something like:
#
# An error occurred while resolving field Query.hello
# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File ".../site-packages/graphql/execution/executor.py", line 200, in resolve_or_error
#     return executor.execute(resolve_fn, source, args, context, info)
#   File ".../site-packages/graphql/execution/executors/asyncio.py", line 50, in execute
#     result = fn(*args, **kwargs)
#   File "test.py", line 11, in resolve_hello
#     raise Exception("wololo")
# Exception: wololo

# As a side note, the produced error have a correct stack trace:
print(result.errors[0].stack) # <traceback object at 0x123456789>

print("=" * 10)
print("Async execution")

async_result = graphql(schema, '{ helloAsync }', executor=AsyncioExecutor())
# While producing the same result, it won't log anything, and good luck to know where
# the error comes from.

# This error has None as a stack trace:
print(async_result.errors[0].stack) # None
```

I am a beginner with python async stuff so I won't send a PR, but as a temporary solution I am replacing the `AsyncioExecutor execute` method with something like that:
```python
    def execute(self, fn, *args, **kwargs):
        result = fn(*args, **kwargs)
        if isinstance(result, Future) or iscoroutine(result):
            async def hop():
                try:
                    await result
                except Exception as e:
                    logger.exception("Exception in %s", fn)
                    raise
            future = ensure_future(hop(), loop=self.loop)
            # future = ensure_future(result, loop=self.loop)
            self.futures.append(future)
            return Promise.resolve(future)
        return result
```

```

issue_title: compilers using pipes.quote() == not Windows compatible. breaks LESSc
issue_body:
In bug #335, a call to quote() was added to better handle variety of filenames. It turns out that quote() is not windows compatible and is not intended to be (neither the shlex version nor the pipes version).

quote() adds single quote characters, windows requires double quotes as single quotes have no special meaning (to windows it's just an apostrophe, and apostrophes are ok in windows filenames).

For me, pipeline is adding single quotes everywhere it calls lessc. This is breaking lessc, which does not handle the single quotes properly. It tries to add the current working directory to the quoted filename and comes up with a brand new filename that is not valid.

To me, things would be much simpler if you called lessc using Popen in 'not shell' mode. Pass in the name of the executable, pass in the arguments as an array. Dont try to quote anything. dont turn the whole thing into one long command string. Is there a reason it was not implemented this way?

I have already been working with lessc. They merged a patch for me which "fixes" the single quote issue. But now we are removing it because it breaks to deviate from the windows specs in this way.

I can submit a PR, just wanted to get feedback first if possible.

wonder if this is related to issue #368 ...

issue_title: How not to have the source files collected in `STATIC_ROOT` ?
issue_body:
Currently upgrading from django-pipeline 1.3.27 to 1.6.6.

The new default configuration works fine except that I ended with too many files collected in `STATIC_ROOT`.

Ok, so heading to the documentation, storage section:

> If you want to exclude Pipelinable content from your collected static files, you can also use Pipeline’s FileSystemFinder and AppDirectoriesFinder. These finders will also exclude unwanted content like READMEs, tests and examples, which are particularly useful if you’re collecting content from a tool like Bower.

Nice, so I'll try using `pipeline.finders.FileSystemFinder` and `pipeline.finders.AppDirectoriesFinder` instead of their django versions but now the compiling / compressing part no longer works because the compilers / compressors are looking for the source files in `STATIC_ROOT` where there are no longer collected as wanted.

So now I collect the files I need + the source files, then run pipeline and finally remove the source files from `STATIC_ROOT` but it just feels *not right*. (plus it's not really fixed, if I have `source.scss` compiled to `source.css` compressed to `output.css`, I'm still ending up with an unwanted `source.css` in `STATIC_ROOT`).

Previously we had `pipeline.storage.PipelineFinderStorage` who used all the finders without the `ignore_patterns` to find and use all the source files no matter if they were collected in `STATIC_ROOT` in the end. This Storage was removed when `pipeline.collector.default_collector` was introduced (I'm still confused about it's purpose) and replaced by django's stock `staticfiles_storage` who cannot find the source files if they're not copied in `STATIC_ROOT`.

Related issues: https://github.com/jazzband/django-pipeline/issues/504 https://github.com/jazzband/django-pipeline/issues/503 but they have not attracted any response.

I'll would be glad if someone could point me in the right direction. Am I wrongly using django-pipeline ? Because if not, at that point I may as well directly fix django-pipeline than hack around it but I would need some guidelines from a maintainer before starting to do so.

issue_title: Projector point isolation : Cosine distance change and explained variance decrease
issue_body:
Hello,
This issue is a request for an explaination about two points in Tensorboard's projector :
#### 1 - Cosine distance changes when isolating a selection of points
Cosine distance seems to change when zooming on points. The right side column showing the nearest neighours has the mention 'in the original space'. I think I'm not the only one who also interpreted it as 'the cosine distance is also calculated in the original space', so I think it should be mentioned that it is not the case. The cosine distance seems to have been mapped from it's original interval to an interval somewhat close to [0, 2], but I couldn't find the formula for this change anywhere.
To sum it up:
- The change occuring in the formula should be mentioned and documented
- We should have a button to enable/disable this modified cosine distance

#### 2 - PCA displays an explained variance of 100.0%, but it decreases when isolating points
Initial PCA with 100% explained variance shows a superposition of points with a cosine distance value of 0.000. When selecting and isolating these points, the superposition is no more, and the explained variance goes down to 92.6%. The superposed points are 20 in a 450 points representation, and there are many of such superposition cases in the same embedding (hence the fact that 100% variance explained is a bit shady.
[Here](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1zd_251M7BYVftu37jKMnXa3CI9oNAca3/view?usp=sharing) is a link to my Tensorboard files folder.
An example of the case of superposition can be found in the embedding _Embedddings/t_embedding_ if you look for the point labeled _technicien assurance qualité_.

Thank you for your time, and pardon my mistakes if I submitted this first issue in the wrong format.

-------------------------

Tensorboard version : 1.8.0
Tensorflow version: 1.6.0
Python Version : 3.6
OS : Windows 10

issue_body:
**Contexto**

Se calculan indicadores "no numéricos" que tienen una dimensión de apertura por alguna variable y cuentan la cantidad de datasets / distribuciones que caen en cada caso.

Los metadatos recomendados u opcionales no siempre tienen un valor (no son obligatorios) y actualmente el análisis de estos indicadores impide estar seguro cómo se compone el universo y cuál es el grado de cobertura de quienes sí responden con ese dato.

**Propuesta**

Incorporar la cuenta de los `None` a los indicadores no numéricos, de forma tal que se cuente cuando el metadato no está presente o tiene valor nulo en un dataset o distribución.

Esto permitiría *(tener en cuenta para tests)* controlar el resultado de los no numéricos: el total de distribuciones debe dar `distribuciones_cant`  y el total de datasets debe dar `datasets_cant`
